The **Portuguese Empire[[a]](#cite_note-name-1)** was the first European [colonial empire](/wiki/Colonial_empire "Colonial empire"), existing between 1415 and 1999. In conjunction with the [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire"), it ushered in the European [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery"). It achieved a global scale, controlling vast portions of the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"), [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") and various islands in [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") and [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania"). It was one of the most powerful empires of the [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period"), while at its greatest extent in 1820, covering 5.5 million square km (2.1 million square miles),[[1]](#cite_note-Taagepera502-2) making it among the [largest empires](/wiki/List_of_largest_empires "List of largest empires") in history. Composed of [colonies](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism"), [factories](/wiki/Factory_(trading_post)#Portuguese_feitorias_(c._1445) "Factory (trading post)"), and later [overseas territories](/wiki/Territory#Overseas_territory "Territory"), it was the longest-lived colonial empire in history, from the [conquest of Ceuta](/wiki/Conquest_of_Ceuta "Conquest of Ceuta") in North Africa in 1415 to the [handover of Macau](/wiki/Handover_of_Macau "Handover of Macau") to [China](/wiki/China "China") in 1999.[[2]](#cite_note-PageSonnenburg481-3)[[3]](#cite_note-Brockeyxv-4)[[4]](#cite_note-JuangMorrissette894-5)

In the wake of the [Reconquista](/wiki/Reconquista "Reconquista"), [Portuguese sailors began exploring](/wiki/Portuguese_maritime_exploration "Portuguese maritime exploration") the coast of Africa and the Atlantic archipelagos in 1418–1419, using recent developments in navigation, cartography, and maritime technology such as the [caravel](/wiki/Caravel "Caravel"), with the aim of finding a sea route to the source of the lucrative [spice trade](/wiki/Spice_trade "Spice trade"). In 1488, [Bartolomeu Dias](/wiki/Bartolomeu_Dias "Bartolomeu Dias") rounded the [Cape of Good Hope](/wiki/Cape_of_Good_Hope "Cape of Good Hope") and [Cape Agulhas](/wiki/Cape_Agulhas "Cape Agulhas"), and in 1498 [Vasco da Gama](/wiki/Vasco_da_Gama "Vasco da Gama") reached India. In 1500, [Pedro Álvares Cabral](/wiki/Pedro_%C3%81lvares_Cabral "Pedro Álvares Cabral"), while on a voyage to India, [reached what would later be Brazil](/wiki/Discovery_of_Brazil "Discovery of Brazil"). Over the following decades, Portuguese sailors continued to explore the coasts and islands of East Asia, establishing [forts](/wiki/List_of_Portuguese_colonial_forts "List of Portuguese colonial forts") and factories as they went. By 1571, a string of naval outposts connected [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon") to [Nagasaki](/wiki/Nagasaki "Nagasaki") along the coasts of Africa, the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"), and [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"). This commercial network and the colonial trade had a substantial positive impact on Portuguese economic growth (1500–1800) when it accounted for about a fifth of Portugal's per-capita income.

When King [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain") (Philip I of Portugal) [seized the Portuguese crown](/wiki/War_of_the_Portuguese_Succession "War of the Portuguese Succession") and Portuguese territories such as Brazil in 1580, there began a 60-year union between Spain and Portugal known to subsequent [historiography](/wiki/Historiography "Historiography") as the [Iberian Union](/wiki/Iberian_Union "Iberian Union"), although the realms continued to have separate administrations. As the King of Spain was also King of Portugal, Portuguese colonies became the subject of attacks by three rival European powers hostile to Spain: the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_colonial_empire "Dutch colonial empire"), [England](/wiki/English_overseas_possessions "English overseas possessions"), and [France](/wiki/French_colonial_empire "French colonial empire"). With its smaller population, Portugal found itself unable to effectively defend its overstretched network of trading posts, and the empire began a long and gradual decline. Eventually, Brazil became the most valuable colony of the second era of empire (1663–1825), until, as part of the wave of [independence movements](/wiki/Decolonization_of_the_Americas "Decolonization of the Americas") that swept the Americas during the early 19th century, it [declared its independence](/wiki/Independence_of_Brazil "Independence of Brazil") in 1822.

The third era of empire covers the final stage of Portuguese colonialism after the independence of Brazil in the 1820s. By then, the colonial possessions had been reduced to [forts and plantations along the African coastline](/wiki/Portuguese_Africa_(disambiguation) "Portuguese Africa (disambiguation)") (expanded inland during the [Scramble for Africa](/wiki/Scramble_for_Africa "Scramble for Africa") in the late 19th century), [Portuguese Timor](/wiki/Portuguese_Timor "Portuguese Timor"), and [enclaves](/wiki/Enclave_and_exclave "Enclave and exclave") in [India](/wiki/Portuguese_India "Portuguese India") and [Macau](/wiki/Portuguese_Macau "Portuguese Macau"). The [1890 British Ultimatum](/wiki/1890_British_Ultimatum "1890 British Ultimatum") led to the contraction of [Portuguese ambitions in Africa](/wiki/Pink_Map "Pink Map"). Under [António de Oliveira Salazar](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_de_Oliveira_Salazar "António de Oliveira Salazar") (in office 1932–1968), the [*Estado Novo* dictatorship](/wiki/Estado_Novo_(Portugal) "Estado Novo (Portugal)") made some ill-fated attempts to cling on to its last remaining colonies. Under the ideology of [pluricontinentalism](/wiki/Pluricontinentalism "Pluricontinentalism"), the regime renamed its colonies "[overseas provinces](/wiki/Overseas_province "Overseas province")" while retaining the [system of forced labour](/wiki/Chibalo "Chibalo"), from which only a small [indigenous élite](/wiki/Assimilado "Assimilado") was normally exempt. In August 1961, the [Dahomey](/wiki/Republic_of_Dahomey "Republic of Dahomey") annexed the [Fort of São João Baptista de Ajudá](/wiki/Fort_of_S%C3%A3o_Jo%C3%A3o_Baptista_de_Ajud%C3%A1 "Fort of São João Baptista de Ajudá"), and in December that year [India annexed Goa, Daman, and Diu](/wiki/Annexation_of_Goa "Annexation of Goa"). The [Portuguese Colonial War](/wiki/Portuguese_Colonial_War "Portuguese Colonial War") in Africa lasted from 1961 until the final overthrow of the *Estado Novo* regime in 1974. The [Carnation Revolution](/wiki/Carnation_Revolution "Carnation Revolution") of April 1974 in Portugal led to the hasty [withdrawal from its African colonies](/wiki/Portuguese_Colonial_War#Aftermath "Portuguese Colonial War") and to the [1975 annexation of Portuguese Timor](/wiki/Indonesian_occupation_of_East_Timor "Indonesian occupation of East Timor") by Indonesia. Decolonisation prompted an exodus of [Portuguese colonial settlers](/wiki/Processo_Revolucion%C3%A1rio_Em_Curso#The_retornados "Processo Revolucionário Em Curso") and [mixed-race people](/wiki/Luso-Africans "Luso-Africans") from the colonies. Portugal returned [Macau](/wiki/Portuguese_Macau "Portuguese Macau") to China in 1999. The only overseas possessions to remain under Portuguese rule, the [Azores and Madeira](/wiki/Macaronesian_Portugal "Macaronesian Portugal"), whose native inhabitants were overwhelmingly Portuguese, had their [constitutional status](/wiki/Political_status_of_the_Azores "Political status of the Azores") changed from "[overseas provinces](/wiki/Overseas_province "Overseas province")" to "[autonomous regions](/wiki/Autonomous_regions "Autonomous regions")". The [Community of Portuguese Language Countries](/wiki/Community_of_Portuguese_Language_Countries "Community of Portuguese Language Countries") (CPLP) is the cultural successor of the Empire, analogous to the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") for countries formerly part of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire").

## Origins (1139–1415)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Origins (1139–1415)")]

Main article: [History of Portugal (1139–1415)](/wiki/History_of_Portugal_(1139%E2%80%931415) "History of Portugal (1139–1415)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Porto_April_2019-7.jpg/250px-Porto_April_2019-7.jpg)](/wiki/File:Porto_April_2019-7.jpg)

The [Conquest of Ceuta](/wiki/Portuguese_conquest_of_Ceuta "Portuguese conquest of Ceuta") on 21 August 1415, led by [Henry the Navigator](/wiki/Prince_Henry_the_Navigator "Prince Henry the Navigator") (1394–1460), marked the beginning of the Portuguese Empire.

The origin of the [Kingdom of Portugal](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Portugal "Kingdom of Portugal") lay in the *[reconquista](/wiki/Reconquista "Reconquista")*, the gradual reconquest of the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") from the [Moors](/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus").[[5]](#cite_note-DiffieWinius301-6) After establishing itself as a separate kingdom in 1139, Portugal completed its reconquest of Moorish territory by reaching [Algarve](/wiki/Algarve "Algarve") in 1249, but its independence continued to be threatened by neighbouring [Castile](/wiki/Crown_of_Castile "Crown of Castile") until the signing of the [Treaty of Ayllón](/wiki/Treaty_of_Ayll%C3%B3n "Treaty of Ayllón") in 1411.[[6]](#cite_note-Newitt2005p15-17-7)

Free from threats to its existence and unchallenged by the wars fought by other European states, Portuguese attention turned overseas and towards a military expedition to the Muslim lands of North Africa.[[7]](#cite_note-Newitt19-8) There were several probable motives for their first attack, on the [Marinid Sultanate](/wiki/Marinid_Dynasty "Marinid Dynasty") (in present-day Morocco). It offered the opportunity to continue the Christian [crusade](/wiki/Crusade "Crusade") against Islam; to the military class, it promised glory on the battlefield and the spoils of war;[[8]](#cite_note-Boxer19-9) and finally, it was also a chance to expand Portuguese trade and to address Portugal's economic decline.[[7]](#cite_note-Newitt19-8)

In 1415 an attack was made on [Ceuta](/wiki/Ceuta "Ceuta"), a strategically located North African Muslim enclave along the Mediterranean Sea, and one of the terminal ports of the trans-Saharan gold and slave trades. The [conquest](/wiki/Conquest_of_Ceuta "Conquest of Ceuta") was a military success, and marked one of the first steps in Portuguese expansion beyond the Iberian Peninsula,[[9]](#cite_note-10) but it proved costly to defend against the Muslim forces that soon besieged it. The Portuguese were unable to use it as a base for further expansion into the hinterland,[[10]](#cite_note-Newitt21-11) and the trans-Saharan caravans merely shifted their routes to bypass Ceuta and/or used alternative Muslim ports.[[11]](#cite_note-DiffieWinius55-12)

## First Empire (1415–1663)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: First Empire (1415–1663)")]

Main articles: [History of Portugal (1415–1578)](/wiki/History_of_Portugal_(1415%E2%80%931578) "History of Portugal (1415–1578)") and [Portuguese discoveries](/wiki/Portuguese_discoveries "Portuguese discoveries")

See also: [European enclaves in North Africa before 1830](/wiki/European_enclaves_in_North_Africa_before_1830 "European enclaves in North Africa before 1830")

Although Ceuta proved to be a disappointment for the Portuguese, the decision was taken to hold it while exploring along the Atlantic African coast.[[11]](#cite_note-DiffieWinius55-12) A key supporter of this policy was [Infante](/wiki/Infante "Infante") [Dom](/wiki/Dom_(title) "Dom (title)") [Henry the Navigator](/wiki/Henry_the_Navigator "Henry the Navigator"), who had been involved in the [capture of Ceuta](/wiki/Conquest_of_Ceuta "Conquest of Ceuta"), and who took the lead role in promoting and financing Portuguese maritime exploration until his death in 1460.[[12]](#cite_note-DiffieWinius56-13) At the time, Europeans did not know what lay beyond [Cape Bojador](/wiki/Cape_Bojador "Cape Bojador") on the African coast. Henry wished to know how far the Muslim territories in Africa extended, and whether it was possible to reach Asia by sea, both to reach the source of the lucrative [spice trade](/wiki/Spice_trade "Spice trade") and perhaps to join forces with the fabled Christian kingdom of [Prester John](/wiki/Prester_John "Prester John") that was rumoured to exist somewhere in the "Indies".[[8]](#cite_note-Boxer19-9)[[13]](#cite_note-Anderson50-14) Under his sponsorship, soon the Atlantic islands of [Madeira](/wiki/Madeira "Madeira") (1419) and [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores") (1427) were reached and started to be settled, producing wheat for export to Portugal.[[14]](#cite_note-Coates60-15)

Soon its ships were bringing into the European market highly valued gold, ivory, pepper, cotton, sugar, and slaves. The slave trade, for example, was conducted by a few dozen merchants in Lisbon. In the process of expanding the trade routes, Portuguese navigators mapped unknown parts of Africa, and began exploring the Indian Ocean. In 1487, an overland expedition by [Pêro da Covilhã](/wiki/P%C3%AAro_da_Covilh%C3%A3 "Pêro da Covilhã") made its way to India, exploring trade opportunities with the Indians and Arabs, and winding up finally in Ethiopia. His detailed report was eagerly read in Lisbon, which became the best-informed centre for global geography and trade routes.[[15]](#cite_note-16)

### Initial African coastline excursions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Initial African coastline excursions")]

Fears of what lay beyond [Cape Bojador](/wiki/Cape_Bojador "Cape Bojador"), and whether it was possible to return once it was passed, were assuaged in 1434 when it was rounded by one of Infante Henry's captains, [Gil Eanes](/wiki/Gil_Eanes "Gil Eanes"). Once this psychological barrier had been crossed, it became easier to probe further along the coast.[[16]](#cite_note-DiffieWinius68-17) In 1443, [Infante Dom Pedro](/wiki/Infante_Pedro,_Duke_of_Coimbra "Infante Pedro, Duke of Coimbra"), Henry's brother and by then regent of the Kingdom, granted him the monopoly of navigation, war and trade in the lands south of Cape Bojador. Later this monopoly would be enforced by the [papal bulls](/wiki/Papal_bull "Papal bull") *[Dum Diversas](/wiki/Dum_Diversas "Dum Diversas")* (1452) and *[Romanus Pontifex](/wiki/Romanus_Pontifex "Romanus Pontifex")* (1455), granting Portugal the trade monopoly for the newly discovered lands.[[17]](#cite_note-Daus33-18) A major advance that accelerated this project was the introduction of the [caravel](/wiki/Caravel "Caravel") in the mid-15th century, a ship that could be sailed closer to the wind than any other in operation in Europe at the time.[[18]](#cite_note-Boxer29-19) Using this new maritime technology, Portuguese navigators reached ever more southerly [latitudes](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"), advancing at an average rate of one degree a year.[[19]](#cite_note-Russell-Wood1998p9-20) [Senegal](/wiki/Senegal "Senegal") and [Cape Verde Peninsula](/wiki/Cape_Verde_Peninsula "Cape Verde Peninsula") were reached in 1445.[[20]](#cite_note-Rodriguez79-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Amina.jpg/250px-Amina.jpg)](/wiki/File:Amina.jpg)

16th century map showing Portuguese claims to Guinea and the castle [São Jorge da Mina](/wiki/Elmina_Castle "Elmina Castle")

The first [feitoria](/wiki/Feitoria "Feitoria") trade post overseas was established in 1445 on the island of [Arguin](/wiki/Arguin "Arguin"), off the coast of Mauritania, to attract Muslim traders and monopolise the business in the routes travelled in North Africa. In 1446, [Álvaro Fernandes](/wiki/%C3%81lvaro_Fernandes "Álvaro Fernandes") pushed on almost as far as present-day [Sierra Leone](/wiki/Sierra_Leone "Sierra Leone"), and the [Gulf of Guinea](/wiki/Gulf_of_Guinea "Gulf of Guinea") was reached in the 1460s.[[21]](#cite_note-DiffieWinius469-22) The [Cape Verde](/wiki/Cape_Verde "Cape Verde") Islands were discovered in 1456 and settled in 1462.

Expansion of [sugarcane](/wiki/Sugarcane "Sugarcane") in Madeira started in 1455, using advisers from [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily") and (largely) [Genoese](/wiki/Republic_of_Genoa "Republic of Genoa") capital to produce the "sweet salt" that was rare in Europe. Already cultivated in [Algarve](/wiki/Algarve "Algarve"), the accessibility of Madeira attracted Genoese and Flemish traders keen to bypass Venetian monopolies. Slaves were used, and the proportion of imported slaves in Madeira reached 10% of the total population by the 16th century.[[22]](#cite_note-23) By 1480 [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") had some seventy ships engaged in the Madeira sugar trade, with the refining and distribution concentrated in Antwerp. By the 1490s Madeira had overtaken Cyprus as a producer of sugar.[[23]](#cite_note-Ponting482-24) The success of sugar merchants such as [Bartolomeo Marchionni](/wiki/Bartolomeo_Marchionni "Bartolomeo Marchionni") would propel the investment in future travels.[[24]](#cite_note-Davis84-25)

In 1469, after prince Henry's death and as a result of meagre returns of the African explorations, King [Afonso V](/wiki/Afonso_V_of_Portugal "Afonso V of Portugal") granted the monopoly of trade in part of the [Gulf of Guinea](/wiki/Gulf_of_Guinea "Gulf of Guinea") to merchant [Fernão Gomes](/wiki/Fern%C3%A3o_Gomes "Fernão Gomes").[[25]](#cite_note-BethencourtCurto232-26) Gomes, who had to explore 100 miles (160 km) of the coast each year for five years, discovered the islands of the Gulf of Guinea, including [São Tomé and Príncipe](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Tom%C3%A9_and_Pr%C3%ADncipe "São Tomé and Príncipe"), and found a thriving [alluvial](/wiki/Alluvial "Alluvial") gold trade among the natives and visiting Arab and Berber traders at the port then named [Mina](/wiki/Elmina "Elmina") (the mine), where he established a trading post.[[26]](#cite_note-White138-27) Trade between Elmina and Portugal grew throughout a decade. During the [War of the Castilian Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Castilian_Succession "War of the Castilian Succession"), a large [Castilian](/wiki/Crown_of_Castile "Crown of Castile") fleet attempted to wrest control of this lucrative trade, but were decisively defeated in the 1478 [Battle of Guinea](/wiki/Battle_of_Guinea "Battle of Guinea"), which firmly established an exclusive Portuguese control. In 1481, the recently crowned [João II](/wiki/John_II_of_Portugal "John II of Portugal") decided to build [São Jorge da Mina](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Jorge_da_Mina "São Jorge da Mina") in order to ensure the protection of this trade, which was held again as a royal monopoly. The equator was crossed by navigators sponsored by Fernão Gomes in 1473 and the [Congo River](/wiki/Congo_River "Congo River") by [Diogo Cão](/wiki/Diogo_C%C3%A3o "Diogo Cão") in 1482. It was during this expedition that the Portuguese first encountered the [Kingdom of Kongo](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kongo "Kingdom of Kongo"), with which it soon developed a rapport.[[27]](#cite_note-GannDuignan273-28) During his 1485–86 expedition, Cão continued to [Cape Cross](/wiki/Cape_Cross "Cape Cross"), in present-day [Namibia](/wiki/Namibia "Namibia"), near the [Tropic of Capricorn](/wiki/Tropic_of_Capricorn "Tropic of Capricorn").[[28]](#cite_note-DiffieWinius156-29)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Portuguese_Morocco.PNG/250px-Portuguese_Morocco.PNG)](/wiki/File:Portuguese_Morocco.PNG)

[Portuguese possessions in Morocco](/wiki/Morocco%E2%80%93Portugal_relations "Morocco–Portugal relations") (1415–1769)

In 1488, [Bartolomeu Dias](/wiki/Bartolomeu_Dias "Bartolomeu Dias") rounded the southern tip of Africa and reached [Great Fish River](/wiki/Great_Fish_River "Great Fish River") on the coast of Africa,[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDiffieWinius1977161-30) proving false the view that had existed since [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") that the Indian Ocean was [land-locked](/wiki/Land-locked "Land-locked"). Simultaneously [Pêro da Covilhã](/wiki/P%C3%AAro_da_Covilh%C3%A3 "Pêro da Covilhã"), travelling secretly overland, had reached [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia"), suggesting that a sea route to the Indies would soon be forthcoming.[[30]](#cite_note-Anderson59-31)

As the Portuguese explored the coastlines of Africa, they left behind a series of [padrões](/wiki/Padr%C3%A3o "Padrão"), stone crosses engraved with the Portuguese coat of arms marking their claims,[[31]](#cite_note-Newitt47-32) and built forts and trading posts. From these bases, they engaged profitably in the slave and gold trades. Portugal enjoyed a virtual monopoly on the African seaborne slave trade for over a century, importing around 800 slaves annually. Most were brought to the Portuguese capital Lisbon, where it is estimated black Africans came to constitute 10 percent of the population.[[32]](#cite_note-Anderson55-33)

### Treaty of Tordesillas (1494)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Treaty of Tordesillas (1494)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Spain_and_Portugal.png/250px-Spain_and_Portugal.png)](/wiki/File:Spain_and_Portugal.png)

The 1494 [Treaty of Tordesillas](/wiki/Treaty_of_Tordesillas "Treaty of Tordesillas") meridian divided the world between the crowns [of Portugal](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Portugal "Kingdom of Portugal") and [of Castile](/wiki/Crown_of_Castile "Crown of Castile").

Main article: [Treaty of Tordesillas](/wiki/Treaty_of_Tordesillas "Treaty of Tordesillas")

[Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus")'s 1492 discovery for Spain of the [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World"), which he believed to be Asia, led to disputes between the Spanish and the Portuguese.[[33]](#cite_note-McAlister73-75-34) These were eventually settled by the [Treaty of Tordesillas](/wiki/Treaty_of_Tordesillas "Treaty of Tordesillas") in 1494, which divided the world outside of Europe in an exclusive [duopoly](/wiki/Duopoly "Duopoly") between the Portuguese and the Spanish along a north–south meridian 370 [leagues](/wiki/League_(unit) "League (unit)"), or 970 miles (1,560 km), west of the Cape Verde islands.[[34]](#cite_note-BethencourtCurto165-35) However, as it was not possible at the time to correctly measure [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude"), the exact boundary was disputed by the two countries until 1777.[[35]](#cite_note-DiffieWinius174-36)

The completion of these negotiations with Spain is one of several reasons proposed by historians for why it took nine years for the Portuguese to follow up on Dias's voyage to the Cape of Good Hope, though it has also been speculated that other voyages were in fact taking place in secret during this time.[[36]](#cite_note-DiffieWinius176-37)[[37]](#cite_note-Boxer36-38) Whether or not this was the case, the long-standing Portuguese goal of finding a sea route to Asia was finally achieved in a [ground-breaking voyage](/wiki/Discovery_of_the_sea_route_to_India "Discovery of the sea route to India") commanded by [Vasco da Gama](/wiki/Vasco_da_Gama "Vasco da Gama").[[38]](#cite_note-DiffieWinius176-185-39)

### The Portuguese enter the Indian Ocean

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: The Portuguese enter the Indian Ocean")]

Main articles: [Portuguese discovery of the sea route to India](/wiki/Portuguese_discovery_of_the_sea_route_to_India "Portuguese discovery of the sea route to India"), [Portuguese India](/wiki/Portuguese_India "Portuguese India"), [Portuguese India Armadas](/wiki/Portuguese_India_Armadas "Portuguese India Armadas"), [Indian Ocean trade § Portuguese period](/wiki/Indian_Ocean_trade#Portuguese_period "Indian Ocean trade"), and [Portuguese presence in Asia](/wiki/Portuguese_presence_in_Asia "Portuguese presence in Asia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Ignoto_portoghese%2C_ritratto_di_un_cavaliere_dell%27ordine_di_cristo%2C_1525-50_ca._02.jpg/250px-Ignoto_portoghese%2C_ritratto_di_un_cavaliere_dell%27ordine_di_cristo%2C_1525-50_ca._02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ignoto_portoghese,_ritratto_di_un_cavaliere_dell%27ordine_di_cristo,_1525-50_ca._02.jpg)

[Vasco da Gama](/wiki/Vasco_da_Gama "Vasco da Gama") (1469–1524)

The squadron of [Vasco da Gama](/wiki/Vasco_da_Gama "Vasco da Gama") left Portugal in 1497, rounded the Cape and continued along the coast of East Africa, where a local pilot was brought on board who guided them across the Indian Ocean, reaching [Calicut](/wiki/Kozhikode "Kozhikode") ([Kozhikode](/wiki/Kozhikode "Kozhikode")), the capital of the kingdom ruled by [Zamorins](/wiki/Zamorin "Zamorin"), in south-western India in May 1498.[[39]](#cite_note-40) The second voyage to India was dispatched in 1500 under [Pedro Álvares Cabral](/wiki/Pedro_%C3%81lvares_Cabral "Pedro Álvares Cabral"). While following the same south-westerly route as Gama across the Atlantic Ocean, Cabral made landfall on the Brazilian coast. This was probably an accidental discovery, but it has been speculated that the Portuguese secretly knew of Brazil's existence and that it lay on their side of the Tordesillas line.[[40]](#cite_note-McAlister75-41) Cabral recommended to the Portuguese King that the land be settled, and two follow up voyages were sent in 1501 and 1503. The land was found to be abundant in *[pau-brasil](/wiki/Paubrasilia "Paubrasilia")*, or brazilwood, from which it later inherited its name, but the failure to find gold or silver meant that for the time being Portuguese efforts were concentrated on India.[[41]](#cite_note-McAlister76-42) In 1502, to enforce its trade monopoly over a wide area of the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean"), the Portuguese Empire created the [cartaz](/wiki/Cartaz "Cartaz") licensing system, granting merchant ships protection against pirates and rival states.[[42]](#cite_note-DiffieWinius274,320-323-43)

Profiting from the rivalry between the [ruler of Kochi](/wiki/Cochin_kingdom "Cochin kingdom") and the [Zamorin](/wiki/Zamorin "Zamorin") of [Calicut](/wiki/History_of_Kozhikode "History of Kozhikode"), the Portuguese were well-received and seen as allies, as they obtained a permit to build the [fort *Immanuel*](/wiki/Fort_Emmanuel "Fort Emmanuel") ([Fort Kochi](/wiki/Fort_Kochi "Fort Kochi")) and a trading post that was the first European settlement in India. They established a trading hub at [Tangasseri](/wiki/Tangasseri "Tangasseri"), [Quilon](/wiki/Quilon "Quilon") (*Coulão*, [Kollam](/wiki/Kollam "Kollam")) city in (1503) in 1502, which became the centre of trade in pepper,[[43]](#cite_note-44) and after founding manufactories at [Cochin](/wiki/Cochin "Cochin") (*Cochim*, Kochi) and [Cannanore](/wiki/Kannur "Kannur") (*Canonor*, Kannur), built a factory at [Quilon](/wiki/Quilon "Quilon") in 1503. In 1505 King [Manuel I of Portugal](/wiki/Manuel_I_of_Portugal "Manuel I of Portugal") appointed [Francisco de Almeida](/wiki/Francisco_de_Almeida "Francisco de Almeida") first [Viceroy](/wiki/Viceroy "Viceroy") of Portuguese India, establishing the Portuguese government in the east. That year the Portuguese also conquered [Kannur](/wiki/Kannur "Kannur"), where they founded [St. Angelo Fort](/wiki/St._Angelo_Fort "St. Angelo Fort"), and [Lourenço de Almeida](/wiki/Louren%C3%A7o_de_Almeida "Lourenço de Almeida") arrived in [Ceylon](/wiki/Ceylon "Ceylon") (modern Sri Lanka), where he discovered the source of [cinnamon](/wiki/Cinnamon "Cinnamon").[[44]](#cite_note-BethencourtCurto207-45) Although [Cankili I](/wiki/Cankili_I "Cankili I") of [Jaffna](/wiki/Jaffna "Jaffna") initially resisted contact with them, the [Jaffna kingdom](/wiki/Jaffna_kingdom "Jaffna kingdom") came to the attention of Portuguese officials soon after for their resistance to missionary activities as well as logistical reasons due to its proximity with [Trincomalee](/wiki/Trincomalee "Trincomalee") harbour among other reasons.[[45]](#cite_note-Abeyasinghe2-46) In the same year, Manuel I ordered Almeida to fortify the Portuguese fortresses in Kerala and within eastern Africa, as well as probe into the prospects of building forts in Sri Lanka and Malacca in response to growing hostilities with Muslims within those regions and threats from the Mamluk sultan.[[46]](#cite_note-Disney2009b128-47)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Portuguese_Carracks_off_a_Rocky_Coast.jpg/250px-Portuguese_Carracks_off_a_Rocky_Coast.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portuguese_Carracks_off_a_Rocky_Coast.jpg)

The carrack *[Santa Catarina do Monte Sinai](/wiki/Santa_Catarina_do_Monte_Sinai "Santa Catarina do Monte Sinai")* epitomised the might and the force of the [Portuguese Armada](/wiki/Portuguese_Navy "Portuguese Navy").

A Portuguese fleet under the command of [Tristão da Cunha](/wiki/Trist%C3%A3o_da_Cunha "Tristão da Cunha") and [Afonso de Albuquerque](/wiki/Afonso_de_Albuquerque "Afonso de Albuquerque") conquered [Socotra](/wiki/Socotra "Socotra") at the entrance of the [Red Sea](/wiki/Red_Sea "Red Sea") in 1506 and [Muscat](/wiki/Muscat,_Oman "Muscat, Oman") in 1507. Having failed to conquer [Ormuz](/wiki/Ormus "Ormus"), they instead followed a strategy intended to close off commerce to and from the Indian Ocean.[[47]](#cite_note-DiffieWinius233,235-48) [Madagascar](/wiki/Madagascar "Madagascar") was partly explored by Cunha, and [Mauritius](/wiki/Mauritius "Mauritius") was discovered by Cunha whilst possibly being accompanied by Albuquerque.[[48]](#cite_note-Macmillan11-49) After the capture of Socotra, Cunha and Albuquerque operated separately. While Cunha travelled India and Portugal for trading purposes, Albuquerque went to India to take over as governor after Almeida's three-year term ended. Almeida refused to turn over power and soon placed Albuquerque under house arrest, where he remained until 1509.[[49]](#cite_note-DiffieWinius237-239-50)

Although requested by Manuel I to further explore interests in Malacca and Sri Lanka, Almeida instead focused on western India, in particular the [Sultanate of Gujarat](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Gujarat "Sultanate of Gujarat") due to his suspicions of traders from the region possessing more power. The [Mamlûk Sultanate](/wiki/Mamluk "Mamluk") sultan [Al-Ashraf Qansuh al-Ghawri](/wiki/Al-Ashraf_Qansuh_al-Ghawri "Al-Ashraf Qansuh al-Ghawri") along with the Gujarati sultanate attacked Portuguese forces in the harbor of [Chaul](/wiki/Battle_of_Chaul "Battle of Chaul"), resulting in the death of [Almeida's son](/wiki/Louren%C3%A7o_de_Almeida "Lourenço de Almeida"). In retaliation, the Portuguese fought and destroyed the Mamluks and Gujarati fleets in the sea [Battle of Diu](/wiki/Battle_of_Diu_(1509) "Battle of Diu (1509)") in 1509.[[50]](#cite_note-Disney2009b128-29-51)

Along with Almeida's initial attempts, Manuel I and his council in Lisbon had tried to distribute power in the Indian Ocean, creating three areas of jurisdiction: Albuquerque was sent to the Red Sea, [Diogo Lopes de Sequeira](/wiki/Diogo_Lopes_de_Sequeira "Diogo Lopes de Sequeira") to South-east Asia, seeking an agreement with the Sultan of Malacca, and Jorge de Aguiar followed by Duarte de Lemos were sent to the area between the Cape of Good Hope and Gujarat.[[51]](#cite_note-DiffieWinius245-247-52) However, such posts were centralised by Afonso de Albuquerque after his succession and remained so in subsequent ruling.[[52]](#cite_note-Subrahmanyam67-83-53)

  [Portuguese discoveries](/wiki/Portuguese_discoveries "Portuguese discoveries") and explorations: first arrival places and dates; main Portuguese [spice trade](/wiki/Spice_trade "Spice trade") routes (blue)

  16th century Portuguese illustration from the [Códice Casanatense](/wiki/C%C3%B3dice_Casanatense "Códice Casanatense"), depicting a Portuguese nobleman with his retinue in India

  16th century heavy Portuguese carrack

### Trade with Maritime Asia, Africa and the Indian Ocean

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Trade with Maritime Asia, Africa and the Indian Ocean")]

Main articles: [Portuguese Macao](/wiki/Portuguese_Macao "Portuguese Macao"), [History of Goa](/wiki/History_of_Goa "History of Goa"), [Portuguese Chittagong](/wiki/Portuguese_Chittagong "Portuguese Chittagong"), [Portuguese Malacca](/wiki/Portuguese_Malacca "Portuguese Malacca"), and [Portuguese Empire in the Indonesian Archipelago](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire_in_the_Indonesian_Archipelago "Portuguese Empire in the Indonesian Archipelago")

#### Goa, Malacca and Southeast Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Goa, Malacca and Southeast Asia")]

See also: [Adil Shahi–Portuguese conflicts](/wiki/Adil_Shahi%E2%80%93Portuguese_conflicts "Adil Shahi–Portuguese conflicts"), [Malay-Portuguese conflicts](/wiki/Malay-Portuguese_conflicts "Malay-Portuguese conflicts"), and [Acehnese-Portuguese conflicts](/wiki/Acehnese-Portuguese_conflicts "Acehnese-Portuguese conflicts")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Retrato_de_Afonso_de_Albuquerque_%28ap%C3%B3s_1545%29_-_Autor_desconhecido.png/250px-Retrato_de_Afonso_de_Albuquerque_%28ap%C3%B3s_1545%29_-_Autor_desconhecido.png)](/wiki/File:Retrato_de_Afonso_de_Albuquerque_(ap%C3%B3s_1545)_-_Autor_desconhecido.png)

Second viceroy of India [Afonso de Albuquerque](/wiki/Afonso_de_Albuquerque "Afonso de Albuquerque"), credited with laying the foundations of Portuguese power in Asia

By the end of 1509, Albuquerque became viceroy of the [East Indies](/wiki/East_Indies "East Indies") with the capital at [Velha Goa](/wiki/Velha_Goa "Velha Goa"), after the [Cape route](/wiki/Cape_route "Cape route") was discovered by Vasco da Gama. In contrast to Almeida, Albuquerque was more concerned with strengthening the navy,[[53]](#cite_note-Disney2009b129-54) as well as being more compliant with the interests of the kingdom.[[54]](#cite_note-DiffieWinius238-55) His first objective was to conquer Goa, due to its strategic location as a defensive fort positioned between Kerala and Gujarat, as well as its prominence for [Arabian horse](/wiki/Arabian_horse "Arabian horse") imports.[[50]](#cite_note-Disney2009b128-29-51)

[The initial capture of Goa](/wiki/Portuguese_Conquest_of_Goa_(1510) "Portuguese Conquest of Goa (1510)") from the [Bijapur sultanate](/wiki/Adil_Shahi_dynasty "Adil Shahi dynasty") in 1510 was soon countered by the Bijapuris, but with the help of Hindu [privateer](/wiki/Privateer "Privateer") [Timoji](/wiki/Timoji "Timoji"), on 25 November of the same year it was recaptured.[[55]](#cite_note-Shastry34-45-56)[[56]](#cite_note-Disney2009b130-57) In Goa, Albuquerque began the first Portuguese [mint](/wiki/Mint_(coin) "Mint (coin)") in India in 1510.[[57]](#cite_note-deSouza220-58) He encouraged Portuguese settlers to marry local women, built a church in honor of [St. Catherine](/wiki/St._Catherine_of_Alexandria "St. Catherine of Alexandria") (as it was recaptured on her feast day), and attempted to build rapport with the Hindus by protecting their temples and reducing their tax requirements.[[56]](#cite_note-Disney2009b130-57) The Portuguese maintained friendly relations with the south Indian Emperors of the [Vijayanagara Empire](/wiki/Vijayanagara_Empire "Vijayanagara Empire").[[58]](#cite_note-Mehta291-59)

In April 1511, Albuquerque sailed to [Malacca](/wiki/Malacca "Malacca") on the [Malay Peninsula](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula"),[[59]](#cite_note-Ricklefs23-60) the largest spice market of the period.[[60]](#cite_note-Kratoska2004p98-61) Though the trade was largely dominated by the Gujarati, other groups such as the Turks, Persians, Armenians, Tamils and [Abyssinians](/wiki/People_of_Ethiopia "People of Ethiopia") traded there.[[60]](#cite_note-Kratoska2004p98-61) Albuquerque targeted Malacca to impede the Muslim and Venetian influence in the spice trade and increase that of Lisbon.[[61]](#cite_note-Gipouloux2011p301-302-62) By July 1511, Albuquerque had captured Malacca and sent [António de Abreu](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_de_Abreu "António de Abreu") and [Francisco Serrão](/wiki/Francisco_Serr%C3%A3o "Francisco Serrão") (along with Ferdinand Magellan) to explore the Indonesian archipelago.[[62]](#cite_note-Newitt2005p78-63)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Iberian_mare_clausum_claims.svg/250px-Iberian_mare_clausum_claims.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Iberian_mare_clausum_claims.svg)

Iberian '[mare clausum](/wiki/Mare_clausum "Mare clausum")' in the Age of Discovery. Albuquerque's strategy to encircle the Indian Ocean is shown.

The Malacca peninsula became the strategic base for Portuguese trade expansion with China and Southeast Asia. A strong gate, called the [A Famosa](/wiki/A_Famosa "A Famosa"), was erected to defend the city and remains.[[63]](#cite_note-Ooi2009p202-64) Learning of Siamese ambitions over Malacca, Albuquerque immediately sent [Duarte Fernandes](/wiki/Duarte_Fernandes "Duarte Fernandes") on a diplomatic mission to the [Kingdom of Siam](/wiki/Ayutthaya_Kingdom "Ayutthaya Kingdom") (modern Thailand), where he was the first European to arrive, establishing amicable relations and trade between both kingdoms.[[64]](#cite_note-Lach520-521-65)[[65]](#cite_note-66)

The Portuguese empire pushed further south and proceeded to discover Timor in 1512. [Jorge de Meneses](/wiki/Jorge_de_Menezes "Jorge de Menezes") discovered [New Guinea](/wiki/New_Guinea "New Guinea") in 1526, naming it the "Island of the Papua".[[66]](#cite_note-QuanchiRobson2005-67) In 1517, [João da Silveira](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_da_Silveira "João da Silveira") commanded a fleet to [Chittagong](/wiki/Chittagong "Chittagong"),[[67]](#cite_note-68) and by 1528, the Portuguese had established a [settlement in Chittagong](/wiki/Portuguese_settlement_in_Chittagong "Portuguese settlement in Chittagong").[[68]](#cite_note-Harris225-69) The Portuguese eventually based their centre of operations along the [Hugli River](/wiki/Hugli_River "Hugli River"), where they encountered Muslims, Hindus, and Portuguese deserters known as *Chatins*.[[69]](#cite_note-70)

#### China and Japan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: China and Japan")]

See also: [Chinese people in Portugal](/wiki/Chinese_people_in_Portugal "Chinese people in Portugal"), [Nanban trade](/wiki/Nanban_trade "Nanban trade"), [Slavery in Portugal](/wiki/Slavery_in_Portugal "Slavery in Portugal"), and [Portuguese Nagasaki](/wiki/Portuguese_Nagasaki "Portuguese Nagasaki")

Jorge Álvares was the first European to reach China by sea, while the Romans were the first overland via Asia Minor.[[70]](#cite_note-71)[[71]](#cite_note-Edmonds2002-72)[[72]](#cite_note-Ward2008-73)[[73]](#cite_note-Gleason2007-74) He was also the first European to discover Hong Kong.[[74]](#cite_note-75)[[75]](#cite_note-76) In 1514, Afonso de Albuquerque, the viceroy of the Estado da India, dispatched [Rafael Perestrello](/wiki/Rafael_Perestrello "Rafael Perestrello") to sail to China in order to pioneer European trade relations with the nation.[[76]](#cite_note-77)[[77]](#cite_note-78)

In their first attempts at obtaining trading posts by force, the Portuguese were defeated by the Ming Chinese at the [Battle of Tunmen](/wiki/Battle_of_Tunmen "Battle of Tunmen") in [Tamão](/wiki/Tam%C3%A3o "Tamão") or [Tuen Mun](/wiki/Tuen_Mun "Tuen Mun"). In 1521, the Portuguese lost two ships at the [Battle of Sincouwaan](/wiki/Battle_of_Sincouwaan "Battle of Sincouwaan") in [Lantau Island](/wiki/Lantau_Island "Lantau Island"). The Portuguese also lost two ships at [Shuangyu](/wiki/Shuangyu "Shuangyu") in 1548 where several Portuguese were captured and near the [Dongshan Peninsula](/wiki/Dongshan_County "Dongshan County"). In 1549 two Portuguese junks and [Galeote Pereira](/wiki/Galeote_Pereira "Galeote Pereira") were captured. During these battles the Ming Chinese captured weapons from the defeated Portuguese which they then reverse engineered and mass-produced in China such as [matchlock](/wiki/Matchlock "Matchlock") [musket](/wiki/Musket "Musket") [arquebuses](/wiki/Arquebus "Arquebus") which they named [bird guns](/wiki/Gun_control_in_China#History "Gun control in China") and [breech-loading swivel guns](/wiki/Breech-loading_swivel_gun "Breech-loading swivel gun") which they named as Folangji ([Frankish](/wiki/Franks#Crusaders_and_other_Western_Europeans_as_"Franks" "Franks")) cannon because the Portuguese were known to the Chinese under the name of Franks at this time. The Portuguese later returned to China peacefully and presented themselves under the name Portuguese instead of Franks in the [Luso-Chinese agreement (1554)](/wiki/Luso-Chinese_agreement_(1554) "Luso-Chinese agreement (1554)") and rented Macau as a trading post from China by paying annual lease of hundreds of silver [taels](/wiki/Taels "Taels") to Ming China.[[78]](#cite_note-79)

Despite initial harmony and excitement between the two cultures, difficulties began to arise shortly afterwards, including misunderstanding, bigotry, and even hostility.[[79]](#cite_note-smithsonianmag.com-80) The Portuguese explorer [Simão de Andrade](/wiki/Fern%C3%A3o_Pires_de_Andrade#Andrade's_brother_and_spoiled_relations "Fernão Pires de Andrade") incited poor relations with China due to his pirate activities, raiding Chinese shipping, attacking a Chinese official, and kidnappings of Chinese. He based himself at Tamao island in a fort. The Chinese claimed that Simão kidnapped Chinese boys and girls to be molested and cannibalised.[[80]](#cite_note-Jesus5-81) The Chinese sent a squadron of junks against Portuguese caravels that succeeded in driving the Portuguese away and reclaiming Tamao. As a result, the Chinese posted an edict banning men with Caucasian features from entering Canton, killing multiple Portuguese there, and driving the Portuguese back to sea.[[81]](#cite_note-Dodge226-82)[[82]](#cite_note-Whiteway339-83)

After the Sultan of Bintan detained several Portuguese under Tomás Pires, the Chinese then executed 23 Portuguese and threw the rest into prison where they resided in squalid, sometimes fatal conditions. The Chinese then massacred Portuguese who resided at [Ningbo](/wiki/Ningbo "Ningbo") and [Fujian](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian") trading posts in 1545 and 1549, due to extensive and damaging raids by the Portuguese along the coast, which irritated the Chinese.[[81]](#cite_note-Dodge226-82) Portuguese pirating was second to [Japanese pirating](/wiki/Japanese_pirates "Japanese pirates") by this period. However, they soon began to shield Chinese junks and a cautious trade began. In 1557 the Chinese authorities allowed the Portuguese to settle in Macau, creating a warehouse in the trade of goods between China, Japan, Goa and Europe.[[81]](#cite_note-Dodge226-82)[[83]](#cite_note-Disney2009b175,184-84)

#### Spice Islands (Moluccas) and Treaty of Zaragoza

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Spice Islands (Moluccas) and Treaty of Zaragoza")]

Main article: [Portuguese Empire in the Indonesian Archipelago](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire_in_the_Indonesian_Archipelago "Portuguese Empire in the Indonesian Archipelago")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Attributed_to_Kano_Sanraku_-_Important_Cultural_Property_Namban_Screens_-_Google_Art_Project2.jpg/250px-Attributed_to_Kano_Sanraku_-_Important_Cultural_Property_Namban_Screens_-_Google_Art_Project2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Attributed_to_Kano_Sanraku_-_Important_Cultural_Property_Namban_Screens_-_Google_Art_Project2.jpg)

Portugal was the first European nation to [establish trade routes with Japan](/wiki/Nanban_trade "Nanban trade") and [China](/wiki/Fern%C3%A3o_Pires_de_Andrade "Fernão Pires de Andrade"). A significant portion of the crews on Portuguese ships on the [Japan voyage](/wiki/Japan_voyage "Japan voyage") were [Indian Catholics](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_India "Catholic Church in India").[[84]](#cite_note-85)

Portuguese operations in Asia did not go unnoticed, and in 1521 [Magellan](/wiki/Ferdinand_Magellan "Ferdinand Magellan") arrived in the region and claimed the Philippines for Spain. In 1525, Spain under Charles V sent an expedition to colonise the [Moluccas islands](/wiki/Moluccas_islands "Moluccas islands"), claiming they were in his zone of the [Treaty of Tordesillas](/wiki/Treaty_of_Tordesillas "Treaty of Tordesillas"), since there was no set limit to the east. The expedition of [García Jofre de Loaísa](/wiki/Garc%C3%ADa_Jofre_de_Loa%C3%ADsa "García Jofre de Loaísa") reached the Moluccas, docking at [Tidore](/wiki/Tidore "Tidore"). With the Portuguese already established in nearby Ternate, conflict was inevitable, leading to nearly a decade of skirmishes. A resolution was reached with the [Treaty of Zaragoza](/wiki/Treaty_of_Zaragoza "Treaty of Zaragoza") in 1529, attributing the Moluccas to Portugal and the Philippines to Spain.[[85]](#cite_note-Ooi2004p1340-86) The Portuguese traded regularly with the [Bruneian Empire](/wiki/Bruneian_Empire "Bruneian Empire") from 1530 and described the capital of Brunei as surrounded by a stone wall.

#### South Asia, Persian Gulf and Red Sea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: South Asia, Persian Gulf and Red Sea")]

See also: [Persian–Portuguese war](/wiki/Persian%E2%80%93Portuguese_war "Persian–Portuguese war"), [Capture of Ormuz (1507)](/wiki/Capture_of_Ormuz_(1507) "Capture of Ormuz (1507)"), [Portuguese invasion of Bahrain](/wiki/Portuguese_invasion_of_Bahrain "Portuguese invasion of Bahrain"), [History of Kuwait](/wiki/History_of_Kuwait "History of Kuwait"), [History of Bahrain](/wiki/History_of_Bahrain "History of Bahrain"), [Qeshm Island](/wiki/Qeshm_Island "Qeshm Island"), [Bandar Abbas](/wiki/Bandar_Abbas "Bandar Abbas"), [Portuguese Oman](/wiki/Portuguese_Oman "Portuguese Oman"), [Portuguese Socotra](/wiki/Portuguese_Socotra "Portuguese Socotra"), [Portuguese Ceylon](/wiki/Portuguese_Ceylon "Portuguese Ceylon"), and [Gujarati-Portuguese conflicts](/wiki/Gujarati-Portuguese_conflicts "Gujarati-Portuguese conflicts")

The Portuguese empire expanded into the Persian Gulf, contesting control of the spice trade with the [Ajuran Empire](/wiki/Ajuran_Sultanate "Ajuran Sultanate") and the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"). Albuquerque [conquered](/wiki/Capture_of_Ormuz_(1507) "Capture of Ormuz (1507)") the [Huwala](/wiki/Huwala "Huwala") state of [Hormuz](/wiki/Ormus "Ormus") at the head of the Persian Gulf in 1515, establishing it as a vassal state. [Aden](/wiki/Aden "Aden"), however, [resisted](/wiki/Siege_of_Aden_(1513) "Siege of Aden (1513)") Albuquerque's expedition in that same year and another attempt by Albuquerque's successor [Lopo Soares de Albergaria](/wiki/Lopo_Soares_de_Albergaria "Lopo Soares de Albergaria") in 1516. In 1521 a force led by [António Correia](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_Correia_(admiral) "António Correia (admiral)") captured [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain "Bahrain"), defeating the [Jabrid](/wiki/Jabrid "Jabrid") King, [Muqrin ibn Zamil](/wiki/Muqrin_ibn_Zamil "Muqrin ibn Zamil").[[86]](#cite_note-87) [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain "Bahrain") was under full Portuguese control by 1521, making it the first Middle Eastern country to be fully colonised by a European power.[[87]](#cite_note-88) In a shifting series of alliances, the Portuguese dominated much of the southern Persian Gulf for the next hundred years. With the regular maritime route linking Lisbon to Goa since 1497, the [island of Mozambique](/wiki/Island_of_Mozambique "Island of Mozambique") became a strategic port, and there was built [Fort São Sebastião](/wiki/Fort_S%C3%A3o_Sebasti%C3%A3o_(Mozambique) "Fort São Sebastião (Mozambique)") and a hospital. In the Azores, the Islands Armada protected the ships en route to Lisbon.[[88]](#cite_note-O'Flanagan125-89)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/The_Portuguese_Fortress_of_Diu_in_the_mid-16th_century.png/250px-The_Portuguese_Fortress_of_Diu_in_the_mid-16th_century.png)](/wiki/File:The_Portuguese_Fortress_of_Diu_in_the_mid-16th_century.png)

The fortress of Diu, one of the most important Portuguese strongholds in India that was [besieged by the Ottomans in 1538](/wiki/Siege_of_Diu_(1538) "Siege of Diu (1538)").

In 1534, Gujarat faced attack from the [Mughals](/wiki/Mughals "Mughals") and the Rajput states of [Chitor](/wiki/Chitor "Chitor") and [Mandu](/wiki/Mandu,_Madhya_Pradesh "Mandu, Madhya Pradesh"). The Sultan [Bahadur Shah of Gujarat](/wiki/Bahadur_Shah_of_Gujarat "Bahadur Shah of Gujarat") was forced to sign the [Treaty of Bassein](/wiki/Treaty_of_Bassein_(1534) "Treaty of Bassein (1534)") with the Portuguese, establishing an alliance to regain the country, giving in exchange [Daman](/wiki/Daman,_Daman_and_Diu "Daman, Daman and Diu"), [Diu](/wiki/Diu,_India "Diu, India"), [Mumbai](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai") and [Bassein](/wiki/Vasai "Vasai"). It also regulated the trade of Gujarati ships departing to the [Red Sea](/wiki/Red_Sea "Red Sea") and passing through Bassein to pay duties and allow the horse trade.[[89]](#cite_note-Pearson1976pp74-82-90) After Mughal ruler [Humayun](/wiki/Humayun "Humayun") had success against Bahadur, the latter signed another treaty with the Portuguese to confirm the provisions and allowed the fort to be built in Diu. Shortly afterward, Humayun turned his attention elsewhere, and the Gujarats allied with the Ottomans to regain control of Diu and lay siege to the fort. The two failed sieges of [1538](/wiki/Siege_of_Diu_(1538) "Siege of Diu (1538)") and [1546](/wiki/Siege_of_Diu_(1546) "Siege of Diu (1546)") put an end to Ottoman ambitions, confirming the Portuguese hegemony in the region,[[89]](#cite_note-Pearson1976pp74-82-90)[[90]](#cite_note-Malekandathil116-118-91) as well as gaining superiority over the Mughals.[[91]](#cite_note-Mathew138-92) However, the Ottomans fought off attacks from the Portuguese in the Red Sea and in the [Sinai Peninsula](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula") in 1541, and in the northern region of the Persian Gulf in 1546 and 1552. Each entity ultimately had to respect the sphere of influence of the other, albeit unofficially.[[92]](#cite_note-93)[[93]](#cite_note-94)

#### Sub-Saharan Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Sub-Saharan Africa")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Theodore_de_Bry_-_Harbour_scene_%28colorized%29.jpg/250px-Theodore_de_Bry_-_Harbour_scene_%28colorized%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Theodore_de_Bry_-_Harbour_scene_(colorized).jpg)

Portuguese [carracks](/wiki/Carracks "Carracks") unload cargo in Lisbon. Original engraving by [Theodor de Bry](/wiki/Theodor_de_Bry "Theodor de Bry"), 1593, coloured at a later date

After a series of prolonged contacts with Ethiopia, the Portuguese embassy made contact with the [Ethiopian (Abyssinian) Kingdom](/wiki/Ethiopian_Empire "Ethiopian Empire") led by Rodrigo de Lima in 1520.[[94]](#cite_note-Abir86-95)[[95]](#cite_note-96) This coincided with the Portuguese search for Prester John, as they soon associated the kingdom as his land.[[96]](#cite_note-97) The fear of Turkish advances within the Portuguese and Ethiopian sectors also played a role in their alliance.[[94]](#cite_note-Abir86-95)[[97]](#cite_note-98) The [Adal Sultanate](/wiki/Adal_Sultanate "Adal Sultanate") defeated the Ethiopians in the [battle of Shimbra Kure](/wiki/Battle_of_Shimbra_Kure "Battle of Shimbra Kure") in 1529, and Islam spread further in the [region](/wiki/Ethiopian_Highlands "Ethiopian Highlands"). Portugal responded by aiding king [Gelawdewos](/wiki/Gelawdewos_of_Ethiopia "Gelawdewos of Ethiopia") with Portuguese soldiers and muskets. Though the Ottomans responded with support of soldiers and muskets to the Adal Sultanate, after the death of the Adali sultan [Ahmad ibn Ibrahim al-Ghazi](/wiki/Ahmad_ibn_Ibrahim_al-Ghazi "Ahmad ibn Ibrahim al-Ghazi") in the [battle of Wayna Daga](/wiki/Battle_of_Wayna_Daga "Battle of Wayna Daga") in 1543, the joint Adal-Ottoman force retreated.[[98]](#cite_note-99)[[99]](#cite_note-Stapleton2013p121-100)[[100]](#cite_note-101)

The Portuguese also made direct contact with the [Kongolose](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kongo "Kingdom of Kongo") vassal state [Ndongo](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ndongo "Kingdom of Ndongo") and its ruler Ngola Kiljuane in 1520, after the latter requested missionaries.[[101]](#cite_note-Mancall2007p207-102) Kongolese king [Afonso I](/wiki/Afonso_I_of_Kongo "Afonso I of Kongo") interfered with the process with denunciations, and later sent a Kongo mission to Ndongo after the latter had arrested the Portuguese mission that came.[[101]](#cite_note-Mancall2007p207-102) The growing official and unofficial slave trading with Ndongo strained relations between Kongo and the Portuguese, and even had Portuguese ambassadors from São Tomé support Ndongo against the Kingdom of Kongo.[[102]](#cite_note-Thornton2000pp100-101-103)[[103]](#cite_note-HeywoodThornton2007p83-104) However, when the [Jaga](/wiki/Jaga_(Kongo) "Jaga (Kongo)") attacked and conquered regions of Kongo in 1568, Portuguese assisted Kongo in their defeat.[[104]](#cite_note-Stapleton2013p175-105) In response, the Kongo allowed the colonisation of Luanda Island; Luanda was established by [Paulo Dias de Novais](/wiki/Paulo_Dias_de_Novais "Paulo Dias de Novais") in 1576 and soon became a slave port.[[104]](#cite_note-Stapleton2013p175-105)[[105]](#cite_note-106) De Novais' subsequent alliance with Ndongo angered Luso-Africans who resented the influence from the Crown.[[106]](#cite_note-HeywoodThornton2007p86-107) In 1579, Ndongo ruler Ngola Kiluanje kia Ndamdi massacred Portuguese and Kongolese residents in the Ndongo capital [Kabasa](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ndongo "Kingdom of Ndongo") under the influence of Portuguese renegades. Both the Portuguese and Kongo fought against Ndongo, and off-and-on warfare between the Ndongo and Portugal would persist for decades.[[107]](#cite_note-Mancall2007pp208-211-108)

In east-Africa, the main agents acting on behalf of the Portuguese Crown, exploring and settling the territory of what would become Mozambique were the **[prazeiros](/wiki/Prazeiros "Prazeiros")**, to whom vast estates around the Zambezi River were leased by the King as a reward for their services. Commanding vast armies of [chikunda](/wiki/Chikunda "Chikunda") warrior-slaves, these men acted as feudal-like lords, either levying tax from local chieftains, defending them and their estates from marauding tribes, participating in the ivory or slave trade, and becoming involved in the politics of the [Kingdom of Mutapa](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Mutapa "Kingdom of Mutapa"), to the point of installing client kings upon its throne.

#### Missionary expeditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Missionary expeditions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Saint_Francis_Xavier_taking_leave_of_King_John_III_%281635%29_-_Jos%C3%A9_Avelar_Rebelo.png/250px-Saint_Francis_Xavier_taking_leave_of_King_John_III_%281635%29_-_Jos%C3%A9_Avelar_Rebelo.png)](/wiki/File:Saint_Francis_Xavier_taking_leave_of_King_John_III_(1635)_-_Jos%C3%A9_Avelar_Rebelo.png)

[St. Francis Xavier](/wiki/St._Francis_Xavier "St. Francis Xavier") requesting [John III of Portugal](/wiki/John_III_of_Portugal "John III of Portugal") for a missionary expedition in Asia

In 1542, Jesuit missionary [Francis Xavier](/wiki/Francis_Xavier "Francis Xavier") arrived in Goa at the service of João III of Portugal, in charge of an [Apostolic Nunciature](/wiki/Apostolic_Nunciature "Apostolic Nunciature"). At the same time [Francisco Zeimoto](/wiki/Francisco_Zeimoto "Francisco Zeimoto"), [António Mota](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_Mota "António Mota"), and other traders arrived in Japan for the first time. According to [Fernão Mendes Pinto](/wiki/Fern%C3%A3o_Mendes_Pinto "Fernão Mendes Pinto"), who claimed to be in this journey, they arrived at [Tanegashima](/wiki/Tanegashima "Tanegashima"), where the locals were impressed by [firearms](/wiki/Tanegashima_(Japanese_matchlock) "Tanegashima (Japanese matchlock)"), that would be immediately made by the Japanese on a large scale.[[108]](#cite_note-Pacey1991-109) By 1570 the Portuguese bought part of a Japanese port where they founded a small part of the city of [Nagasaki](/wiki/Nagasaki "Nagasaki"),[[109]](#cite_note-110) and it became the major trading port in Japan in the triangular trade with China and Europe.[[110]](#cite_note-Disney2009b195-111)

Guarding its trade from both European and Asian competitors, Portugal dominated not only the trade between Asia and Europe, but also much of the trade between different regions of Asia and Africa, such as India, Indonesia, China, and Japan. [Jesuit](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus") missionaries, followed the Portuguese to spread [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism")[[111]](#cite_note-112)[[112]](#cite_note-113) to Asia and Africa with mixed success.[[113]](#cite_note-BethencourtCurto262-265-114)

### Colonisation efforts in the Americas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Colonisation efforts in the Americas")]

Main article: [Portuguese colonisation of the Americas](/wiki/Portuguese_colonisation_of_the_Americas "Portuguese colonisation of the Americas")

#### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Canada")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Terra_Nova_e_Labrador_01.png/250px-Terra_Nova_e_Labrador_01.png)](/wiki/File:Terra_Nova_e_Labrador_01.png)

The Portuguese mapped and claimed [Canada](/wiki/Portugal_Cove-St._Philip%27s "Portugal Cove-St. Philip's") in 1499 and the 1500s.

Based on the [Treaty of Tordesillas](/wiki/Treaty_of_Tordesillas "Treaty of Tordesillas"), the Portuguese Crown, under the kings Manuel I, João III and Sebastão, also claimed territorial rights in North America (reached by [John Cabot](/wiki/John_Cabot "John Cabot") in 1497 and 1498). To that end, in 1499 and 1500, [João Fernandes Lavrador](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_Fernandes_Lavrador "João Fernandes Lavrador") explored [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland") and the north Atlantic coast of Canada, which accounts for the appearance of "Labrador" on topographical maps of the period.[[114]](#cite_note-DiffieWinius464-115) Subsequently, in 1500–1501 and 1502, the brothers [Gaspar](/wiki/Gaspar_Corte-Real "Gaspar Corte-Real") and [Miguel Corte-Real](/wiki/Miguel_Corte-Real "Miguel Corte-Real") explored what is today the Canadian province of [Newfoundland and Labrador](/wiki/Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Newfoundland and Labrador"), and Greenland, claiming these lands for Portugal. In 1506, King Manuel I created taxes for the cod fisheries in Newfoundland waters.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Around 1521, [João Álvares Fagundes](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_%C3%81lvares_Fagundes "João Álvares Fagundes") was granted [donatary](/wiki/Donat%C3%A1rio "Donatário") rights to the inner islands of the [Gulf of St. Lawrence](/wiki/Gulf_of_St._Lawrence "Gulf of St. Lawrence") and also created a settlement on [Cape Breton Island](/wiki/Cape_Breton_Island "Cape Breton Island") to serve as a base for cod fishing. Pressure from natives and competing European fisheries prevented a permanent establishment and it was abandoned five years later. Several attempts to establish settlements in Newfoundland over the next half-century also failed.[[115]](#cite_note-Disney2009a116-116)

#### Brazil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Brazil")]

Within a few years after Cabral arrived from Brazil, competition came along from France. In 1503, an expedition under the command of [Gonçalo Coelho](/wiki/Gon%C3%A7alo_Coelho "Gonçalo Coelho") reported French raids on the Brazilian coasts,[[116]](#cite_note-HerringHerring214-117) and explorer [Binot Paulmier de Gonneville](/wiki/Binot_Paulmier_de_Gonneville "Binot Paulmier de Gonneville") traded for brazilwood after making contact in southern Brazil a year later.[[117]](#cite_note-118) Expeditions sponsored by [Francis I](/wiki/Francis_I_of_France "Francis I of France") along the North American coast directly violated of the [Treaty of Tordesilhas](/wiki/Treaty_of_Tordesilhas "Treaty of Tordesilhas").[[118]](#cite_note-PickettPickett14-119) By 1531, the French had stationed a trading post off of an island on the Brazilian coast.[[118]](#cite_note-PickettPickett14-119)

The increase in brazilwood smuggling from the French led [João III](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_III "João III") to press an effort to establish effective occupation of the territory.[[119]](#cite_note-Marley2008p76-120) In 1531, a royal expedition led by [Martim Afonso de Sousa](/wiki/Martim_Afonso_de_Sousa "Martim Afonso de Sousa") and his brother Pero Lopes went to patrol the whole Brazilian coast, banish the French, and create some of the first colonial towns – among them [São Vicente](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Vicente,_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Vicente, São Paulo"), in 1532.[[120]](#cite_note-Marley2008pp76-78-121) Sousa returned to Lisbon a year later to become [governor of India](/wiki/List_of_governors_of_Portuguese_India "List of governors of Portuguese India") and never returned to Brazil.[[121]](#cite_note-DeOliveira254-122)[[122]](#cite_note-Marley2008p78-123) The French attacks did cease to an extent after retaliation led to the Portuguese paying the French to stop attacking Portuguese ships throughout the Atlantic,[[118]](#cite_note-PickettPickett14-119) but the attacks would continue to be a problem well into the 1560s.[[123]](#cite_note-Marley2005pp694-696-124)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Capitanias.jpg/250px-Capitanias.jpg)](/wiki/File:Capitanias.jpg)

A map from 1574 showing the 15 hereditary [captaincy colonies of Brazil](/wiki/Colonial_Brazil "Colonial Brazil")

Upon Sousa's arrival and success, fifteen latitudinal tracts, theoretically to span from the coast to the Tordesillas limit, were decreed by João III on 28 September 1532.[[121]](#cite_note-DeOliveira254-122)[[124]](#cite_note-Marley2005p694-125) The plot of the lands formed the hereditary [captaincies](/wiki/Colonial_Brazil#Captaincies "Colonial Brazil") (Capitanias Hereditárias) to grantees rich enough to support settlement, as had been done successfully in [Madeira](/wiki/Madeira "Madeira") and [Cape Verde](/wiki/Colonial_Cape_Verde "Colonial Cape Verde") islands.[[125]](#cite_note-DiffieWinius310-126) Each [captain-major](/wiki/Captain-major "Captain-major") was to build settlements, grant allotments and administer justice, being responsible for developing and taking the costs of colonisation, although not being the owner: he could transmit it to offspring, but not sell it. Twelve recipients came from Portuguese gentry who become prominent in Africa and India and senior officials of the court, such as [João de Barros](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_de_Barros "João de Barros").[[126]](#cite_note-deAbreu1998-127)

Of the fifteen original captaincies, only two, [Pernambuco](/wiki/Captaincy_of_Pernambuco "Captaincy of Pernambuco") and [São Vicente](/wiki/Captaincy_of_S%C3%A3o_Vicente "Captaincy of São Vicente"), prospered.[[127]](#cite_note-DeOliveira255-56-128) Both were dedicated to the crop of [sugar cane](/wiki/Sugar_cane "Sugar cane"), and the settlers managed to maintain alliances with [Native Americans](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas"). The rise of the sugar industry came about because the Crown took the easiest sources of profit (brazilwood, spices, etc.), leaving settlers to come up with new revenue sources.[[128]](#cite_note-DeOliveira255-129) The establishment of the sugar cane industry demanded intensive labour that would be met with Native American and, later, African slaves.[[129]](#cite_note-BethencourtCurto111-119-130) Deeming the *capitanias* system ineffective, João III decided to centralise the government of the colony in order to "give help and assistance" to grantees. In 1548 he created the first General Government, sending in [Tomé de Sousa](/wiki/Tom%C3%A9_de_Sousa "Tomé de Sousa") as first governor and selecting a capital at the [Bay of All Saints](/wiki/Bay_of_All_Saints "Bay of All Saints"), making it at the [Captaincy of Bahia](/wiki/Captaincy_of_Bahia "Captaincy of Bahia").[[130]](#cite_note-Lockhart190-191-131)[[131]](#cite_note-Bakewell496-132)

Tomé de Sousa built the capital of Brazil, [Salvador](/wiki/Salvador,_Bahia "Salvador, Bahia"), at the Bay of All Saints in 1549.[[132]](#cite_note-Mahoney246-133) Among Sousa's 1,000 man expedition were soldiers, workers, and six [Jesuits](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus") led by [Manuel da Nóbrega](/wiki/Manuel_da_N%C3%B3brega "Manuel da Nóbrega").[[133]](#cite_note-Russell-Wood1968p47-134) The Jesuits would have an essential role in the settlement of Brazil, including the cities of São Vicente, and [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo"), the latter co-founded by Nóbrega.[[134]](#cite_note-Ladle185-135) Along with the Jesuit missions later came disease among the natives, among them [plague](/wiki/Plague_(disease) "Plague (disease)") and [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox").[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*][[135]](#cite_note-136) Subsequently, the French would resettle in Portuguese territory at [Guanabara Bay](/wiki/Guanabara_Bay "Guanabara Bay"), which would be called [France Antarctique](/wiki/France_Antarctique "France Antarctique").[[136]](#cite_note-Marley2008p86-137) While a Portuguese ambassador was sent to [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") to report the French intrusion, João III appointed [Mem de Sá](/wiki/Mem_de_S%C3%A1 "Mem de Sá") as new Brazilian governor general, and Sá left for Brazil in 1557.[[136]](#cite_note-Marley2008p86-137) Sá and his forces had by 1560 expelled the combined [Huguenot](/wiki/Huguenot "Huguenot"), Scottish [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinist "Calvinist"), and slave forces from France Antarctique, but left survivors after burning their fortifications and villages. These survivors would settle [Gloria Bay](/wiki/Gl%C3%B3ria,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Glória, Rio de Janeiro"), [Flamengo Beach](/wiki/Flamengo,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Flamengo, Rio de Janeiro"), and [Parapapuã](/wiki/Governador_Island "Governador Island") with the assistance of the [Tamoio](/wiki/Tup%C3%AD_people "Tupí people") natives.[[137]](#cite_note-Marley2008p90-138)

The Tamoio had been allied with the French since the settlement of France Antarctique, and despite the French loss in 1560, the Tamoio were still a threat.[[138]](#cite_note-Treece31-139) They launched two attacks in 1561 and 1564 (the latter event was assisting the French), and were nearly successful with each.[[139]](#cite_note-Marley2008pp91-92-140)[[140]](#cite_note-141) By this time period, Manuel de Nóbrega, along with fellow Jesuit [José de Anchieta](/wiki/Joseph_of_Anchieta "Joseph of Anchieta"), took part as members of attacks on the Tamoios and as spies for their resources.[[138]](#cite_note-Treece31-139)[[139]](#cite_note-Marley2008pp91-92-140) From 1565 through 1567 Mem de Sá and his forces eventually destroyed France Antarctique at Guanabara Bay. He and his nephew, [Estácio de Sá](/wiki/Est%C3%A1cio_de_S%C3%A1 "Estácio de Sá"), then established the city of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro") in 1567, after Mem de Sá proclaimed the area "São Sebastião do Rio de Janeiro" in 1565.[[141]](#cite_note-Marley2008p96-142) By 1575, the Tamoios had been subdued and essentially were extinct,[[138]](#cite_note-Treece31-139) and by 1580 the government became more of an *ouvidor general* rather than the *ouvidores*.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*][[142]](#cite_note-Schwartz1973p41-143)

### Iberian Union, Protestant rivalry, and colonial stasis (1580–1663)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Iberian Union, Protestant rivalry, and colonial stasis (1580–1663)")]

Main articles: [Iberian Union](/wiki/Iberian_Union "Iberian Union"), [Council of Portugal](/wiki/Council_of_Portugal "Council of Portugal"), [Dutch-Portuguese War](/wiki/Dutch-Portuguese_War "Dutch-Portuguese War"), and [Portuguese Restoration War](/wiki/Portuguese_Restoration_War "Portuguese Restoration War")

See also: [War of the Portuguese Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Portuguese_Succession "War of the Portuguese Succession")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Philip_II%27s_realms_in_1598.png/250px-Philip_II%27s_realms_in_1598.png)](/wiki/File:Philip_II%27s_realms_in_1598.png)

The [Iberian Union](/wiki/Iberian_Union "Iberian Union") in 1598, during the reign of [Philip I and II](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain"), King of Portugal and Spain

In 1580, King [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain") invaded Portugal after a [crisis of succession](/wiki/1580_Portuguese_succession_crisis "1580 Portuguese succession crisis") brought about by king [Sebastião of Portugal](/wiki/Sebastian,_King_of_Portugal "Sebastian, King of Portugal")'s death during a disastrous [Portuguese attack on Alcácer Quibir](/wiki/Battle_of_Alc%C3%A1cer_Quibir "Battle of Alcácer Quibir") in [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco") in 1578. At the Cortes of Tomar in 1581, Philip was crowned Philip I of Portugal, uniting the two crowns and overseas empires under [Spanish Habsburg](/wiki/Spanish_Habsburgs "Spanish Habsburgs") rule in a [dynastic](/wiki/Dynastic_union "Dynastic union") [Iberian Union](/wiki/Iberian_Union "Iberian Union").[[143]](#cite_note-Kamen177-144) At Tomar, Philip promised to keep the empires legally distinct, leaving the administration of the Portuguese Empire to Portuguese nationals, with a [Viceroy of Portugal](/wiki/List_of_Viceroys_of_Portugal_during_the_Iberian_Union "List of Viceroys of Portugal during the Iberian Union") in Lisbon seeing to his interests.[[144]](#cite_note-Boyajian11-145) Philip even had the capital moved to Lisbon for a two-year period (1581–83) due to it being the most important city in the [Iberian peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_peninsula "Iberian peninsula").[[145]](#cite_note-Gallagher8-146) All the Portuguese colonies accepted the new state of affairs except for the [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores"), which held out for [António](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio,_Prior_of_Crato "António, Prior of Crato"), a Portuguese rival claimant to the throne who had garnered the support of [Catherine de Medici](/wiki/Catherine_de_Medici "Catherine de Medici") of France in exchange for the promise to cede Brazil. Spanish forces eventually captured the islands in 1583.[[146]](#cite_note-Anderson104-105-147)

The Tordesillas boundary between Spanish and Portuguese control in South America was then increasingly ignored by the Portuguese, who pressed beyond it into the heart of Brazil,[[144]](#cite_note-Boyajian11-145) allowing them to expand the territory to the west. Exploratory missions were carried out both ordered by the government, the "entradas" (entries), and by private initiative, the "bandeiras" (flags), by the "[bandeirantes](/wiki/Bandeirantes "Bandeirantes")".[[147]](#cite_note-Boxer386-148) These expeditions lasted for years venturing into unmapped regions, initially to capture natives and force them into slavery, and later focusing on finding gold, silver and diamond mines.[[148]](#cite_note-BethencourtCurto111,117-149)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/La_recuperaci%C3%B3n_de_Bah%C3%ADa%2C_Ma%C3%ADno.jpg/250px-La_recuperaci%C3%B3n_de_Bah%C3%ADa%2C_Ma%C3%ADno.jpg)](/wiki/File:La_recuperaci%C3%B3n_de_Bah%C3%ADa,_Ma%C3%ADno.jpg)

[The Recovery of São Salvador da Bahia de Todos os Santos](/wiki/The_Recovery_of_Bah%C3%ADa_de_Todos_los_Santos "The Recovery of Bahía de Todos los Santos"), by [Philip III of Portugal](/wiki/Philip_III_of_Portugal "Philip III of Portugal"), from the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic")

However, the union meant that Spain dragged Portugal into its conflicts with England, France and the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic"), countries which were beginning to establish their own overseas empires.[[149]](#cite_note-Anderson105-150) The primary threat came from the Dutch, who had been engaged in a [struggle for independence](/wiki/Eighty_Years_War "Eighty Years War") against Spain since 1568. In 1581, the [Seven Provinces](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") gained independence from the [Habsburg rule](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg"), leading Philip II to prohibit commerce with Dutch ships, including in Brazil where Dutch had invested large sums in financing sugar production.[[150]](#cite_note-Thomas159-151)

Spanish imperial trade networks now were opened to Portuguese merchants, which was particularly lucrative for Portuguese slave traders who could now sell slaves in Spanish America at a higher price than could be fetched in Brazil.[[151]](#cite_note-Lockhart250-152) In addition to this newly acquired access to the Spanish *[asientos](/wiki/Asiento "Asiento")*, the Portuguese were able to solve their bullion shortage issues with access to the production of the silver mining in Peru and Mexico.[[152]](#cite_note-Newitt163-153) [Manila](/wiki/Manila "Manila") was also incorporated into the Macau-Nagasaki trading network, allowing Macanese of Portuguese descent to act as trading agents for Philippine Spaniards and use Spanish silver from the Americas in trade with China, and they later drew competition with the Dutch East India Company.[[153]](#cite_note-Disney2009b186-154)

In 1592, during the [war with Spain](/wiki/Anglo-Spanish_War_(1585%E2%80%931604) "Anglo-Spanish War (1585–1604)"), an English fleet [captured](/wiki/Battle_of_Flores_(1592) "Battle of Flores (1592)") a large Portuguese carrack off the Azores, the [Madre de Deus](/wiki/Madre_de_Deus "Madre de Deus"), which was loaded with 900 tons of merchandise from India and China estimated at half a million [pounds](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling") (nearly half the size of English Treasury at the time).[[154]](#cite_note-155) This foretaste of the riches of the East galvanised English interest in the region.[[155]](#cite_note-156) That same year, [Cornelis de Houtman](/wiki/Cornelis_de_Houtman "Cornelis de Houtman") was sent by Dutch merchants to Lisbon, to gather as much information as he could about the Spice Islands.[[153]](#cite_note-Disney2009b186-154)[[156]](#cite_note-Lach200-157)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Rua_Direita_na_Cidade_de_Goa.jpg/250px-Rua_Direita_na_Cidade_de_Goa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rua_Direita_na_Cidade_de_Goa.jpg)

[Joannes van Doetecum](/wiki/Joannes_van_Doetecum_the_Elder "Joannes van Doetecum the Elder")'s 1596 print of "[The Market of Goa](/wiki/Market_of_Goa_(Van_Doetecum) "Market of Goa (Van Doetecum)")" in [Linschoten](/wiki/Jan_Huygen_van_Linschoten "Jan Huygen van Linschoten")'s *Itinerario*, showing the main street of Portuguese Goa in the 1580s

The Dutch eventually came to acknowledge the importance of Goa in breaking up the Portuguese empire in Asia. In 1583, merchant and explorer [Jan Huyghen van Linschoten](/wiki/Jan_Huyghen_van_Linschoten "Jan Huyghen van Linschoten") (1563 – 8 February 1611), formerly the Dutch secretary of the Archbishop of Goa, had acquired information while serving in that position that contained the location of secret Portuguese trade routes throughout Asia, including those to the East Indies and Japan. It was published in 1595 and then greatly expanded the next year as his *Itinerario*.[[157]](#cite_note-158)[[158]](#cite_note-159) Dutch and English interests used this new information, leading to their commercial expansion, including the foundation of the English [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") in 1600, and the Dutch East India Company in 1602. These developments allowed the entry of [chartered companies](/wiki/Chartered_companies "Chartered companies") into the East Indies.[[159]](#cite_note-Crow1992-160)[[160]](#cite_note-BowenLincoln2002-161)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Victor_Meirelles_-_%27Battle_of_Guararapes%27%2C_1879%2C_oil_on_canvas%2C_Museu_Nacional_de_Belas_Artes%2C_Rio_de_Janeiro.JPG/250px-Victor_Meirelles_-_%27Battle_of_Guararapes%27%2C_1879%2C_oil_on_canvas%2C_Museu_Nacional_de_Belas_Artes%2C_Rio_de_Janeiro.JPG)](/wiki/File:Victor_Meirelles_-_%27Battle_of_Guararapes%27,_1879,_oil_on_canvas,_Museu_Nacional_de_Belas_Artes,_Rio_de_Janeiro.JPG)

The Portuguese victory at the [Second Battle of Guararapes](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_Guararapes "Second Battle of Guararapes") ended Dutch presence in [Pernambuco](/wiki/Pernambuco "Pernambuco").

The Dutch took their fight overseas, attacking Spanish and Portuguese colonies and beginning the [Dutch–Portuguese War](/wiki/Dutch%E2%80%93Portuguese_War "Dutch–Portuguese War"), which would last for over sixty years (1602–1663). Other European nations, such as Protestant England, assisted the [Dutch Empire](/wiki/Dutch_Empire "Dutch Empire") in the war. The Dutch attained victories in Asia and Africa with assistance of various indigenous allies, eventually wrenching control of [Malacca](/wiki/Portuguese_Malacca "Portuguese Malacca") (1641), [Portuguese Gold Coast](/wiki/Portuguese_Gold_Coast "Portuguese Gold Coast") (1642; [Elmina Castle](/wiki/Elmina_Castle "Elmina Castle") seized by the Dutch already in 1637), [Ceylon](/wiki/Portuguese_Ceylon "Portuguese Ceylon") (1658), and [Kochi](/wiki/Kochi "Kochi") (1663). The Dutch also had regional control of the lucrative sugar-producing region of [northeast Brazil](/wiki/Dutch_Brazil "Dutch Brazil") as well as [Luanda](/wiki/Portuguese_Angola "Portuguese Angola"), but the Portuguese regained these territories after considerable struggle.[[161]](#cite_note-Disney2009b73-74,168-171,226-231-162)[[162]](#cite_note-163)

Meanwhile, in the Persian Gulf region, the Portuguese also lost control of [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain "Bahrain") in 1602, [Ormuz](/wiki/Capture_of_Ormuz_(1622) "Capture of Ormuz (1622)") was captured by a joint alliance of the [Safavids](/wiki/Safavid_Empire "Safavid Empire") and the English in 1622, and [Muscat](/wiki/Muscat,_Oman "Muscat, Oman") was captured by [Oman](/wiki/Oman "Oman") under the [Al-Ya'arubs](/wiki/Yaruba_dynasty "Yaruba dynasty") in 1650.[[163]](#cite_note-Disney2009b169-164) The [1625 Battle off Hormuz](/wiki/Battle_off_Hormuz_(1625) "Battle off Hormuz (1625)"), one of the most important of the [Portuguese–Safavid wars](/wiki/Portuguese%E2%80%93Safavid_wars "Portuguese–Safavid wars"), would result in a draw.[[164]](#cite_note-WF-165) They would continue to use Muscat as a base for repetitive incursions within the Indian Ocean, [including capturing Fort Jesus in 1698](/wiki/Siege_of_Fort_Jesus "Siege of Fort Jesus"), which resulted in a loss of [Zanzibar](/wiki/Zanzibar "Zanzibar") in 1698.[[165]](#cite_note-Disney2009b350-166) In Ethiopia and Japan in the 1630s, the ousting of missionaries by local leaders severed influence in the respective regions.[[166]](#cite_note-167)[[167]](#cite_note-168)

## Second Empire (1663–1822)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Second Empire (1663–1822)")]
