Further information: [History of Portugal (1640–1777)](/wiki/History_of_Portugal_(1640%E2%80%931777) "History of Portugal (1640–1777)") and [History of Portugal (1777–1834)](/wiki/History_of_Portugal_(1777%E2%80%931834) "History of Portugal (1777–1834)")

The loss of colonies was one of the reasons that contributed to the end of the personal union with Spain. João IV was proclaimed king of Portugal in 1640 and the [Portuguese Restoration War](/wiki/Portuguese_Restoration_War "Portuguese Restoration War") began. Even before the war's final resolution, the crown established the Overseas Council, conceived in 1642 on the short-lived model of the Council of India (1604–1614), and established in 1643, it was the governing body for most of the Portuguese overseas empire. The exceptions were North Africa, Madeira, and the Azores. All correspondence concerning overseas possessions were funneled through the council. When the Portuguese court fled to Brazil in 1807, following the Napoleonic invasion of Iberia, Brazil was removed from the jurisdiction of the council. It made recommendations concerning personnel for the administrative, fiscal, and military, as well as bishops of overseas dioceses.[[168]](#cite_note-169) A distinguished seventeenth-century member was [Salvador de Sá](/wiki/Salvador_de_S%C3%A1 "Salvador de Sá").[[169]](#cite_note-170)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Nau_Rainha_de_Portugal.jpg/250px-Nau_Rainha_de_Portugal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nau_Rainha_de_Portugal.jpg)

Portuguese warship *Rainha de Portugal* saluting the squadron of Admiral Ball at Malta in 1798. In the 18th century the [Portuguese Navy](/wiki/Portuguese_Navy "Portuguese Navy") was among the most powerful in the world.

In 1661 the Portuguese offered [Bombay](/wiki/Bombay "Bombay") and [Tangier](/wiki/Portuguese_Tangier "Portuguese Tangier") to England as part of a dowry, and over the next hundred years the English gradually became the dominant trader in India, gradually excluding the trade of other powers. Spain recognised the end of the [Iberian Union](/wiki/Iberian_Union "Iberian Union") in 1668, with Portugal in exchange ceding [Ceuta](/wiki/Ceuta "Ceuta") to the Spanish crown.[[170]](#cite_note-Cowans2003-171)

After the Portuguese were defeated by the Indian rulers [Chimnaji Appa](/wiki/Chimnaji_Appa "Chimnaji Appa") of the [Maratha Empire](/wiki/Maratha_Confederacy "Maratha Confederacy")[[171]](#cite_note-172)[[172]](#cite_note-Ahmed330-173) and by [Shivappa Nayaka](/wiki/Shivappa_Nayaka "Shivappa Nayaka") of the [Keladi Nayaka Kingdom](/wiki/Keladi_Nayaka_Kingdom "Keladi Nayaka Kingdom")[[173]](#cite_note-174) and at the end of confrontations with the Dutch, Portugal was only able to cling onto [Goa](/wiki/Goa "Goa") and several minor bases in India, and managed to regain territories in Brazil and Africa, but lost forever to prominence in Asia as trade was diverted through increasing numbers of English, Dutch and French trading posts. In 1787, in [Goa](/wiki/Goa "Goa") the [Conspiracy of the Pintos](/wiki/Conspiracy_of_the_Pintos "Conspiracy of the Pintos"), also known as the Pinto Revolt, known in Portuguese as *A Conjuração dos Pintos* occurred, this was a rebellion against [Portuguese](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") rule.[[174]](#cite_note-175)

The leaders of the plot were three prominent priests from the village of [Candolim](/wiki/Candolim "Candolim") in the **[concelho](/wiki/Concelho "Concelho")** of [Bardez](/wiki/Bardez "Bardez"), Goa. They belonged to the noble Pinto clan, hence the name of the rebellion.[[175]](#cite_note-176)

This was fought due to the Portuguese refusing to create bishops and other officials from the colonies and demanded equality. The family was one of the first [Indian](/wiki/People_from_India "People from India") families to be considered as a [Fidalgo](/wiki/Fidalgo "Fidalgo") by the Portuguese crown and two brothers were granted a coat of arms in 1770, and included in the [Portuguese nobility](/wiki/Portuguese_nobility "Portuguese nobility")

This was the first anti-colonial revolt in [India](/wiki/India "India") and one of the first by [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") subjects in all European colonies.

Thus, throughout the century, Brazil gained increasing importance to the empire, which exported [brazilwood](/wiki/Brazilwood "Brazilwood") and sugar.[[148]](#cite_note-BethencourtCurto111,117-149)

### Minas Gerais and the gold industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Minas Gerais and the gold industry")]

In 1693, the Bandeirante Antônio Rodrigues de Arzão, [Caipira](/wiki/Caipiras "Caipiras") from [Taubaté](/wiki/Taubat%C3%A9 "Taubaté"), discovered gold in the interior of the [Captaincy of São Vicente](/wiki/Captaincy_of_S%C3%A3o_Vicente "Captaincy of São Vicente"), an area currently corresponding to [Minas Gerais](/wiki/Minas_Gerais "Minas Gerais"), in Brazil.[[176]](#cite_note-177) Major discoveries of gold and, later, diamonds in Minas Gerais, Mato Grosso and Goiás led to a "[gold rush](/wiki/Brazil_Gold_Rush "Brazil Gold Rush")", with a large influx of migrants.[[177]](#cite_note-Boxer168-178) The village became the new economic focal point of the empire, with rapid settlement and some conflicts. This gold cycle led to the creation of an internal market and attracted a large number of immigrants. In 1739, when Minas Gerais was already separated as a captaincy, its population was somewhere between 200,000 and 250,000.[[178]](#cite_note-Disney2009b268-69-179)

The gold rush considerably increased the revenue of the Portuguese crown, who charged a fifth of all the ore mined, or the "fifth". Diversion and smuggling were frequent, along with altercations between [Paulistas](/wiki/Paulistas "Paulistas") and *Emboabas* (Term of the [Paulista General Language](/wiki/Paulista_General_Language "Paulista General Language") and [Caipira dialect](/wiki/Caipira_dialect "Caipira dialect") that refers to 'foreigner', used at the time to refer to immigrants from Portugal and other regions in Brazil). In 1709, after the defeat of the Bandeirantes in the [War of the Emboabas](/wiki/War_of_the_Emboabas "War of the Emboabas"), which culminated in the bankruptcy and sale of the Captaincy of São Vicente to the Portuguese crown, which would reorganize it as the Captaincy of São Paulo and Minas de Ouro, a whole set of bureaucratic controls began.[[179]](#cite_note-Bethell203-180)[[180]](#cite_note-Disney2009b267-68-181) The crown also restricted the diamond mining within its jurisdiction and to private contractors.[[180]](#cite_note-Disney2009b267-68-181) In spite of gold increasing global commerce, the plantation industry became the leading export for Brazil during this period; sugar constituted at 50% of the exports (with gold at 46%) in 1760.[[178]](#cite_note-Disney2009b268-69-179)

Africans became the largest group of people in Minas Gerais. Slaves labeled as *[Minas](/wiki/Mina_(Brazil) "Mina (Brazil)")* and *Angolas* rose in high demand during the boom. The [Akan](/wiki/Akan_peoples "Akan peoples") within the *Minas* group had a reputation to have been experts in extrapolating gold in their native regions, and became the preferred group. In spite of the high death rate associated with the slaves involved in the mining industry, the owners that allowed slaves that extracted above the minimum amount of gold to keep the excesses, which in turn led to the possibility of [manumission](/wiki/Manumission "Manumission"). Those that became free partook in artisan jobs such as cobblers, tailors, and blacksmiths. In spite of free blacks and [mulattoes](/wiki/Mulattoes "Mulattoes") playing a large role in Minas Gerais, the number of them that received marginalisation was greater there than in any other region in Brazil.[[181]](#cite_note-Disney2009b273-275-182)

Gold discovered in Mato Grosso and Goiás sparked an interest to solidify the western borders of the colony. In the 1730s contact with Spanish outposts occurred more frequently, and the Spanish threatened to launch a military expedition in order to remove them. This failed to happen and by the 1750s the Portuguese were able to implant a political stronghold in the region.[[182]](#cite_note-Disney2009b288-183)

In 1755 Lisbon suffered a catastrophic [earthquake](/wiki/1755_Lisbon_earthquake "1755 Lisbon earthquake"), which together with a subsequent [tsunami](/wiki/Tsunami "Tsunami") killed between 40,000 and 60,000 people out of a population of 275,000.[[183]](#cite_note-KozakCermak131-184) This sharply checked Portuguese colonial ambitions in the late 18th century.[[184]](#cite_note-Ramasamy2006-185)

According to economic historians, Portugal's colonial trade had a substantial positive impact on Portuguese economic growth, 1500–1800. Leonor Costa et al. conclude:

> intercontinental trade had a substantial and increasingly positive impact on economic growth. In the heyday of colonial expansion, eliminating the economic links to empire would have reduced Portugal's per capita income by roughly a fifth. While the empire helped the domestic economy it was not sufficient to annul the tendency towards decline in relation to Europe's advanced core which set in from the 17th century onwards.[[185]](#cite_note-186)

### Pombaline and post-Pombaline Brazil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Pombaline and post-Pombaline Brazil")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Mapa_da_cidade_do_Rio_de_Janeiro.jpg/250px-Mapa_da_cidade_do_Rio_de_Janeiro.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mapa_da_cidade_do_Rio_de_Janeiro.jpg)

Plan of the city of São Sebastião (Rio de Janeiro) in 1820

Unlike Spain, Portugal did not divide its [colonial territory in America](/wiki/Colonial_Brazil "Colonial Brazil"). The [captaincies](/wiki/Capitania "Capitania") created there functioned under a centralised administration in [Salvador](/wiki/Salvador_da_Bahia "Salvador da Bahia"), which reported directly to the Crown in Lisbon. The 18th century was marked by increasing centralisation of royal power throughout the Portuguese empire. The [Jesuits](/wiki/Jesuits "Jesuits"), who protected the natives against slavery, were brutally suppressed by the [Marquis of Pombal](/wiki/Marquis_of_Pombal "Marquis of Pombal"), which led to the dissolution of the order in the region by 1759.[[186]](#cite_note-Disney2009a298-302-187) Pombal wished to improve the status of the natives by declaring them free and increasing the [mestizo](/wiki/Mestizo "Mestizo") population by encouraging intermarriage between them and the white population. Indigenous freedom decreased in contrast to its period under the Jesuits, and the response to intermarriage was lukewarm at best.[[187]](#cite_note-Disney2009b288-89-188) The crown's revenue from gold declined and plantation revenue increased by the time of Pombal, and he made provisions to improve each. Although he failed to spike the gold revenue, two short-term companies he established for the plantation economy drove a significant increase in production of cotton, rice, cacao, tobacco, and sugar. Slavery increased as well as involvement from the textile economy. The economic development as a whole was inspired by elements of the [Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") in mainland Europe.[[188]](#cite_note-Disney2009b277-79,_283-84-189) However, the diminished influence from states such as the United Kingdom increased the Kingdom's dependence upon Brazil.[[189]](#cite_note-190)

Encouraged by the example of the United States of America, which had won its independence from Britain, the colonial province of [Minas Gerais](/wiki/Minas_Gerais "Minas Gerais") attempted to achieve the same objective in 1789. However, the [Inconfidência Mineira](/wiki/Inconfid%C3%AAncia_Mineira "Inconfidência Mineira") failed, its leaders were arrested, and of the participants in the insurrections, the one of lowest social position, [Tiradentes](/wiki/Tiradentes "Tiradentes"), was hanged.[[190]](#cite_note-Bethell163-164-191) Among the conspiracies led by the African population was the Bahian revolt in 1798, led primarily by João de Deus do Nascimento. Inspired by the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), leaders proposed a society without slavery, food prices would be lowered, and trade restriction abolished. Impoverished social conditions and a high cost of living were among reasons of the revolt. Authorities diffused the plot before major action began; they executed four of the conspirators and exiled several others to the Atlantic Coast of Africa.[[191]](#cite_note-Disney2009b296-297-192) Several more smaller-scale slave rebellions and revolts would occur from 1801 and 1816 and fears within Brazil were that these events would lead to a ["second Haiti"](/wiki/Haitian_Revolution "Haitian Revolution").[[192]](#cite_note-193)

In spite of the conspiracies, the rule of Portugal in Brazil was not under serious threat. Historian A. R. Disney states that the colonists did not until the transferring of the Kingdom in 1808 assert influence of policy changing due to direct contact,[[193]](#cite_note-Disney2009b298-194) and historian Gabriel Paquette mentions that the threats in Brazil were largely unrealised in Portugal until 1808 because of effective policing and espionage.[[194]](#cite_note-195) More revolts would occur after the arrival of the court.[[195]](#cite_note-196)

### Brazilian Independence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Brazilian Independence")]

Further information: [Independence of Brazil](/wiki/Independence_of_Brazil "Independence of Brazil"); [Transfer of the Portuguese Court to Brazil](/wiki/Transfer_of_the_Portuguese_Court_to_Brazil "Transfer of the Portuguese Court to Brazil"); and [United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Portugal,_Brazil_and_the_Algarves "United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Pedro_Am%C3%A9rico_-_Independ%C3%AAncia_ou_Morte_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Pedro_Am%C3%A9rico_-_Independ%C3%AAncia_ou_Morte_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pedro_Am%C3%A9rico_-_Independ%C3%AAncia_ou_Morte_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

[Brazilian independence](/wiki/Independence_of_Brazil "Independence of Brazil") crippled the Portuguese Empire, both economically and politically, for a long time

In 1808, [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte") invaded Portugal, and [Dom João](/wiki/John_VI_of_Portugal "John VI of Portugal"), [prince regent](/wiki/Prince_regent "Prince regent") in place of his mother, [Queen Maria I](/wiki/Maria_I_of_Portugal "Maria I of Portugal"), ordered the transfer of the royal court to Brazil. In 1815 Brazil was elevated to the status of Kingdom, the Portuguese state officially becoming the [United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Portugal,_Brazil_and_the_Algarves "United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves") (*Reino Unido de Portugal, Brasil e Algarves*), and the capital was transferred from Lisbon to Rio de Janeiro, the only instance of a European country being ruled from one of its colonies. There was also the election of Brazilian representatives to the [Cortes Constitucionais Portuguesas](/wiki/Portuguese_Cortes "Portuguese Cortes") (Portuguese Constitutional Courts), the Parliament that assembled in Lisbon in the wake of the [Liberal Revolution of 1820](/wiki/Liberal_Revolution_of_1820 "Liberal Revolution of 1820").[[196]](#cite_note-Bethell177-178-197)

Although the royal family returned to Portugal in 1821, the interlude led to a growing desire for independence amongst Brazilians. In 1822, the son of Dom João VI, then prince-regent [Dom Pedro I](/wiki/Dom_Pedro_I "Dom Pedro I"), proclaimed the independence of Brazil on 7 September 1822, and was crowned Emperor of the new [Empire of Brazil](/wiki/Empire_of_Brazil "Empire of Brazil"). Unlike the Spanish colonies of South America, Brazil's independence was achieved without significant bloodshed.[[197]](#cite_note-Angus1837-198)[[198]](#cite_note-Levine2003-199)

## Third empire (1822–1999)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Third empire (1822–1999)")]

Main articles: [Portuguese Mozambique](/wiki/Portuguese_Mozambique "Portuguese Mozambique"), [Portuguese Angola](/wiki/Portuguese_Angola "Portuguese Angola"), [Portuguese Guinea](/wiki/Portuguese_Guinea "Portuguese Guinea"), and [Portuguese Timor](/wiki/Portuguese_Timor "Portuguese Timor")

Further information: [Liberal Wars](/wiki/Liberal_Wars "Liberal Wars"), [History of Portugal (1834–1910)](/wiki/History_of_Portugal_(1834%E2%80%931910) "History of Portugal (1834–1910)"), and [Campaigns of Pacification and Occupation](/wiki/Campaigns_of_Pacification_and_Occupation "Campaigns of Pacification and Occupation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Jesuit_Convent%2C_Macao.jpg/250px-Jesuit_Convent%2C_Macao.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jesuit_Convent,_Macao.jpg)

The façade of [St. Paul's College](/wiki/St._Paul%27s_College,_Macau "St. Paul's College, Macau") in [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau"), 1854

At the height of [European colonialism](/wiki/European_colonialism "European colonialism") in the 19th century, Portugal had lost its territory in [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America") and all but a few bases in Asia. During this phase, Portuguese colonialism focused on expanding its outposts in Africa into nation-sized territories to compete with other European powers there. Portugal pressed into the hinterland of Angola and Mozambique, and explorers [Serpa Pinto](/wiki/Alexandre_de_Serpa_Pinto "Alexandre de Serpa Pinto"), [Hermenegildo Capelo](/wiki/Hermenegildo_Capelo "Hermenegildo Capelo") and [Roberto Ivens](/wiki/Roberto_Ivens "Roberto Ivens") were among the first Europeans to cross Africa west to east.[[199]](#cite_note-Newitt1995pp335-336-200)[[200]](#cite_note-Corrado18-201)

Slavery was officially abolished in the Portuguese Empire in 1878. However, this did not end [forced labour](/wiki/Forced_labour "Forced labour") or imply equality between "civilized" and "indigenous" subjects of the empire. This distinction would even be legally codified in a more explicit way by the [First Republic](/wiki/First_Portuguese_Republic "First Portuguese Republic") which [took power](/wiki/5_October_1910_revolution "5 October 1910 revolution") in 1910.[[201]](#cite_note-202)[[202]](#cite_note-203)

### British Ultimatum and end of Portuguese monarchy (1890–1910)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: British Ultimatum and end of Portuguese monarchy (1890–1910)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Chaimite.jpg/250px-Chaimite.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chaimite.jpg)

In the 19th century, Portugal launched campaigns to solidify Portuguese Africa.

The project to connect the two colonies, the [Pink Map](/wiki/Pink_Map "Pink Map"), was the main objective of Portuguese policy in the 1880s.[[203]](#cite_note-Lisboa134-204) However, the idea was unacceptable to the British, who had their own aspirations of contiguous British territory running from [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo") to [Cape Town](/wiki/Cape_Town "Cape Town"). The [1890 British Ultimatum](/wiki/1890_British_Ultimatum "1890 British Ultimatum") was reluctantly accepted by [Carlos I of Portugal](/wiki/Carlos_I_of_Portugal "Carlos I of Portugal") and the Pink Map came to an end.[[203]](#cite_note-Lisboa134-204)

The King's reaction to the ultimatum was exploited by republicans.[[203]](#cite_note-Lisboa134-204) On 1 February 1908, King Carlos and Prince [Luís Filipe](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_Filipe,_Prince_Royal_of_Portugal "Luís Filipe, Prince Royal of Portugal") were [assassinated](/wiki/Lisbon_Regicide "Lisbon Regicide") in Lisbon by two Portuguese republican activist revolutionaries, [Alfredo Luís da Costa](/wiki/Alfredo_Lu%C3%ADs_da_Costa "Alfredo Luís da Costa") and [Manuel Buíça](/wiki/Manuel_Bu%C3%AD%C3%A7a "Manuel Buíça"). Luís Filipe's brother, Manuel, became King [Manuel II of Portugal](/wiki/Manuel_II_of_Portugal "Manuel II of Portugal"). Two years later, on 5 October 1910, he was overthrown and fled into exile in England in Fulwell Park, Twickenham near London and Portugal became a [republic](/wiki/Republic "Republic").[[204]](#cite_note-Wheeler48-61-205)

### World War I

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: World War I")]

Main articles: [German campaign in Angola](/wiki/German_campaign_in_Angola "German campaign in Angola"), [East African Campaign (World War I)](/wiki/East_African_Campaign_(World_War_I) "East African Campaign (World War I)"), [Portugal during World War I](/wiki/Portugal_during_World_War_I "Portugal during World War I"), and [Battle of the Lys (1918)](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Lys_(1918) "Battle of the Lys (1918)")

In 1914, the German Empire formulated plans to usurp Angola from Portuguese control.[[205]](#cite_note-Vincent-Smith1974-206) Skirmishes between Portuguese and German soldiers ensued, resulting in reinforcements being sent from the mainland.[[206]](#cite_note-207) The main objective of these soldiers was to recapture the [Kionga Triangle](/wiki/Kionga_Triangle "Kionga Triangle"), in northern Mozambique, the territory having been subjugated by Germany.
In 1916, after Portugal interned German ships in Lisbon, Germany declared war on Portugal. Portugal followed suit, thus entering World War I.[[207]](#cite_note-Wheeler128-208)
Early in the war, Portugal was involved mainly in supplying the Allies positioned in France. In 1916, there was only one attack on the Portuguese territory, in [Madeira](/wiki/Madeira "Madeira").[[208]](#cite_note-209) In 1917, one of the actions taken by Portugal was to assist Britain in its timber industry, imperative to the war effort. Along with the [Canadian Forestry Corps](/wiki/Canadian_Forestry_Corps "Canadian Forestry Corps"), Portuguese personnel established logging infrastructure in an area now referred to as the "[Portuguese Fireplace](/wiki/Portuguese_Fireplace "Portuguese Fireplace")".[[209]](#cite_note-210)

Throughout 1917 Portugal dispatched contingents of troops to the Allied front in France. Midway in the year, Portugal suffered its first World War I casualty. In Portuguese Africa, Portugal and the British fought numerous battles against the Germans in both Mozambique and Angola. Later in the year, [U-boats](/wiki/U-boats "U-boats") entered Portuguese waters again and once more attacked Madeira, sinking multiple Portuguese ships. Through the beginning of 1918, Portugal continued to fight along the Allied front against Germany, including participation in the infamous [Battle of La Lys](/wiki/Battle_of_La_Lys "Battle of La Lys").[[210]](#cite_note-211) As autumn approached, Germany found success in both Portuguese Africa and against Portuguese vessels, sinking multiple ships. After nearly three years of fighting (from a Portuguese perspective), World War I ended, with an armistice being signed by Germany. At the [Versailles Conference](/wiki/Paris_Peace_Conference,_1919 "Paris Peace Conference, 1919"), Portugal regained control of all its lost territory, but did not retain possession (by the principle of [uti possidetis](/wiki/Uti_possidetis "Uti possidetis")) of territories gained during the war, except for [Kionga](/wiki/Kionga "Kionga"), a port city in modern-day [Tanzania](/wiki/Tanzania "Tanzania").[[211]](#cite_note-212)

Portuguese territories in Africa eventually included the modern nations of [Cape Verde](/wiki/Cape_Verde "Cape Verde"), [São Tomé and Príncipe](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Tom%C3%A9_and_Pr%C3%ADncipe "São Tomé and Príncipe"), [Guinea-Bissau](/wiki/Guinea-Bissau "Guinea-Bissau"), [Angola](/wiki/Angola "Angola"), and [Mozambique](/wiki/Mozambique "Mozambique").[[212]](#cite_note-Koffi2012-213)

## Decolonisation and decline (1954–1999)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Decolonisation and decline (1954–1999)")]

Main articles: [Portuguese Colonial War](/wiki/Portuguese_Colonial_War "Portuguese Colonial War"), [Carnation Revolution](/wiki/Carnation_Revolution "Carnation Revolution"), [Transfer of sovereignty over Macau](/wiki/Transfer_of_sovereignty_over_Macau "Transfer of sovereignty over Macau"), and [Annexation of Goa](/wiki/Annexation_of_Goa "Annexation of Goa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Ant%C3%B3nio_de_Oliveira_Salazar_portrait_%28by_Manuel_Alves_San_Payo%29_%E2%80%93_Lisboa.jpg/250px-Ant%C3%B3nio_de_Oliveira_Salazar_portrait_%28by_Manuel_Alves_San_Payo%29_%E2%80%93_Lisboa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ant%C3%B3nio_de_Oliveira_Salazar_portrait_(by_Manuel_Alves_San_Payo)_%E2%80%93_Lisboa.jpg)

Dictatorial prime minister [António de Oliveira Salazar](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_de_Oliveira_Salazar "António de Oliveira Salazar") (r. 1932–1968) sought the preservation of a [pluricontinental](/wiki/Pluricontinentalim "Pluricontinentalim") Portugal

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Ant%C3%B3nio_Guterres_2021.jpg/250px-Ant%C3%B3nio_Guterres_2021.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ant%C3%B3nio_Guterres_2021.jpg)

[António Guterres](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_Guterres "António Guterres") (r. 1995–2002), prime minister by the time of the 1999 [handover of Macau](/wiki/Handover_of_Macau "Handover of Macau"); the Empire's end

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Portuguese_Empire_20th_century.png/250px-Portuguese_Empire_20th_century.png)](/wiki/File:Portuguese_Empire_20th_century.png)

By the 20th century, Portugal no longer called itself an empire, but a [pluricontinental](/wiki/Pluricontinental "Pluricontinental") nation with overseas provinces.

In the wake of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), decolonisation movements began to gain momentum in the empires of the European powers. The ensuing [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") also created instabilities among Portuguese overseas populations, as the United States and Soviet Union vied to increase their spheres of influence. Following the granting of independence to India by Britain in 1947, and the decision by France to allow its [enclaves in India](/wiki/French_India "French India") to be incorporated into the newly independent nation, pressure was placed on Portugal to do the same.[[213]](#cite_note-214) This was resisted by [António de Oliveira Salazar](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_de_Oliveira_Salazar "António de Oliveira Salazar"), who had taken power in 1933. Salazar rebuffed a request in 1950 by [Indian Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_India "Prime Minister of India") [Jawaharlal Nehru](/wiki/Jawaharlal_Nehru "Jawaharlal Nehru") to return the enclaves, viewing them as integral parts of Portugal.[[214]](#cite_note-215) The following year, the Portuguese constitution was amended to change the status of the colonies to overseas provinces. In 1954, a local uprising resulted in the overthrow of the Portuguese authorities in the Indian enclave of [Dadra and Nagar Haveli](/wiki/Dadra_and_Nagar_Haveli "Dadra and Nagar Haveli"). The existence of the remaining Portuguese colonies in India became increasingly untenable and Nehru enjoyed the support of almost all the Indian domestic political parties as well as the Soviet Union and its allies. In 1961, shortly after an uprising against the Portuguese in Angola, Nehru ordered the Indian Army into [Goa](/wiki/Goa "Goa"), [Daman and Diu](/wiki/Daman_and_Diu "Daman and Diu"), which were quickly [captured](/wiki/1961_Indian_Annexation_of_Goa "1961 Indian Annexation of Goa") and formally annexed the following year. Salazar refused to recognise the transfer of sovereignty, considering the territories merely occupied. The Province of Goa continued to be represented in the Portuguese National Assembly until 1974.[[215]](#cite_note-Anderson153-216)

The outbreak of violence in February 1961 in Angola was the beginning of the end of Portugal's empire in Africa. Portuguese army officers in Angola held the view that it would be incapable of dealing militarily with an outbreak of guerilla warfare and therefore that negotiations should begin with the independence movements. However, Salazar publicly stated his determination to keep the empire intact, and by the end of the year, 50,000 troops had been stationed there. The same year, the tiny Portuguese fort of São João Baptista de Ajudá in [Ouidah](/wiki/Ouidah "Ouidah"), a remnant of the West African slave trade, was annexed by the new government of Dahomey (now [Benin](/wiki/Benin "Benin")) that had gained its independence from France. Unrest spread from Angola to Guinea, which rebelled in 1963, and Mozambique in 1964.[[215]](#cite_note-Anderson153-216) Oil deposits found of the coast of [Cabinda](/wiki/Cabinda_Province "Cabinda Province") in the 1966 were used to fund the wars against independence movements.[[216]](#cite_note-:8-217)

According to one historian, Portuguese rulers were unwilling to meet the demands of their colonial subjects in part because Portuguese elites believed that "Portugal lacked the means to conduct a successful "exit strategy" (akin to the "neocolonial" approach followed by the British, the French, or the Belgians)" and in part due to the lack of "a free and open debate [in Salazar's dictatorial state] on the costs of upholding an empire against the anti-colonial consensus that had prevailed in the United Nations since the early 1960s".[[217]](#cite_note-218)
The rise of Soviet influence among the [Movimento das Forças Armadas](/wiki/Movimento_das_For%C3%A7as_Armadas "Movimento das Forças Armadas")'s military (MFA) and working class, and the cost and unpopularity of the [Portuguese Colonial War](/wiki/Portuguese_Colonial_War "Portuguese Colonial War") (1961–1974), in which Portugal resisted to the emerging nationalist guerrilla movements in some of its African territories, eventually led to the collapse of the [Estado Novo](/wiki/Estado_Novo_(Portugal) "Estado Novo (Portugal)") regime in 1974. Known as the "[Carnation Revolution](/wiki/Carnation_Revolution "Carnation Revolution")", one of the first acts of the MFA-led government which then came into power – the [National Salvation Junta](/wiki/National_Salvation_Junta "National Salvation Junta") (*Junta de Salvação Nacional*) – was to end the wars and negotiate Portuguese withdrawal from its African colonies. These events prompted a mass exodus of Portuguese citizens from Portugal's African territories (mostly from [Angola](/wiki/Overseas_Province_of_Angola "Overseas Province of Angola") and [Mozambique](/wiki/Overseas_Province_of_Mozambique "Overseas Province of Mozambique")), creating over a million Portuguese refugees – the *retornados*.[[218]](#cite_note-219) Portugal's new ruling authorities also recognised Goa and other Portuguese India's territories [invaded by India's military forces](/wiki/Invasion_of_Goa "Invasion of Goa"), as Indian territories. Benin's claims over [São João Baptista de Ajudá](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Jo%C3%A3o_Baptista_de_Ajud%C3%A1 "São João Baptista de Ajudá") were accepted by Portugal in 1974.[[219]](#cite_note-220)

Civil wars in [Angola](/wiki/Angolan_Civil_War "Angolan Civil War") and [Mozambique](/wiki/Mozambican_Civil_War "Mozambican Civil War") promptly broke out, with incoming communist governments formed by the former rebels (and backed by the Soviet Union, Cuba, and other communist countries) fighting against insurgent groups supported by nations like [Zaire](/wiki/Zaire "Zaire"), South Africa, and the United States.[[220]](#cite_note-ArnoldWiener11-12-221) [East Timor](/wiki/East_Timor "East Timor") also declared independence in 1975 by making an exodus of many Portuguese refugees to Portugal, which was also known as *retornados*. However, East Timor was almost immediately [invaded by Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesian_invasion_of_East_Timor "Indonesian invasion of East Timor"), which later [occupied](/wiki/Indonesian_occupation_of_East_Timor "Indonesian occupation of East Timor") it until 1999. A [United Nations-sponsored referendum](/wiki/East_Timor_Special_Autonomy_Referendum "East Timor Special Autonomy Referendum") resulted in a majority of East Timorese choosing independence, which was finally achieved in 2002.[[221]](#cite_note-222)

In 1987, Portugal signed the [Sino-Portuguese Joint Declaration](/wiki/Joint_Declaration_on_the_Question_of_Macau "Joint Declaration on the Question of Macau") with the People's Republic of China to establish the process and conditions for the transfer of sovereignty of [Macau](/wiki/Portuguese_Macau "Portuguese Macau"), its last remaining overseas possession. While this process was similar to [the agreement between the United Kingdom and China](/wiki/Sino-British_Joint_Declaration "Sino-British Joint Declaration") two years earlier regarding [Hong Kong](/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong"), the Portuguese transfer to China was met with less resistance than that of Britain regarding Hong Kong, as Portugal had already recognised Macau as Chinese territory under Portuguese administration in 1979.[[222]](#cite_note-223) Under the transfer agreement, Macau is to be governed under a [one country, two systems](/wiki/One_country,_two_systems "One country, two systems") policy, in which it will retain a high degree of autonomy and maintain its capitalist way of life for at least 50 years after the handover, until 2049. The [handover of Macau](/wiki/Transfer_of_sovereignty_over_Macau "Transfer of sovereignty over Macau") on 20 December 1999 officially marked the end of the Portuguese Empire and the end of colonialism in Asia.[[223]](#cite_note-224)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_Empire&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Legacy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Belem_-_Padrao_dos_Descobrimentos.jpg/250px-Belem_-_Padrao_dos_Descobrimentos.jpg)](/wiki/File:Belem_-_Padrao_dos_Descobrimentos.jpg)

[Monument of the Discoveries](/wiki/Monument_of_the_Discoveries "Monument of the Discoveries") in Lisbon

Presently, the [Community of Portuguese Language Countries](/wiki/Community_of_Portuguese_Language_Countries "Community of Portuguese Language Countries") (CPLP) serves as the cultural and [intergovernmental](/wiki/Intergovernmental_organization "Intergovernmental organization") successor of the Empire.[[224]](#cite_note-CPLP_NewsID46-225)

Macau was returned to China on 20 December 1999, under the terms of an agreement negotiated between People's Republic of China and Portugal twelve years earlier. Nevertheless, the Portuguese language remains co-official with [Cantonese Chinese](/wiki/Cantonese_Chinese "Cantonese Chinese") in Macau.[[225]](#cite_note-226)

The [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores") and [Madeira](/wiki/Madeira "Madeira") (the latter administering the uninhabited [Savage Islands](/wiki/Savage_Islands "Savage Islands")) are the only [overseas territories](/wiki/Autonomous_Regions_of_Portugal "Autonomous Regions of Portugal") that remain politically linked to Portugal. Although Portugal began the process of decolonising [East Timor](/wiki/Portuguese_Timor "Portuguese Timor") in 1975, during 1999–2002 was sometimes considered Portugal's last remaining colony, as the [Indonesian invasion of East Timor](/wiki/Indonesian_invasion_of_East_Timor "Indonesian invasion of East Timor") was not justified by Portugal.[[226]](#cite_note-227)

Eight of the former colonies of Portugal have [Portuguese as their official language](/wiki/Geographic_distribution_of_Portuguese "Geographic distribution of Portuguese"). Together with Portugal, they are now members of the [Community of Portuguese Language Countries](/wiki/Community_of_Portuguese_Language_Countries "Community of Portuguese Language Countries"), which when combined total 10,742,000 km2, or 7.2% of the Earth's landmass (148 939 063 km2).[[227]](#cite_note-228) As at 2023, there were 32 associate observers of the CPLP, reflecting the global reach and influence of Portugal's former empire. Moreover, twelve candidate countries or regions have applied[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] for membership to the CPLP and are awaiting approval.[[228]](#cite_note-229)

Portuguese is one of the world's major languages, ranked sixth overall with approximately 240 million speakers around the globe.[[229]](#cite_note-língua_portuguesa-230) It is the third-most-spoken language in the Americas, mainly due to Brazil, although there are also significant communities of Lusophones in nations such as Canada, the US and Venezuela. In addition, there are numerous [Portuguese-based creole languages](/wiki/Portuguese-based_creole_languages "Portuguese-based creole languages"), including the one spoken by the [Kristang](/wiki/Kristang_people "Kristang people") people in [Malacca](/wiki/Malacca "Malacca").[[230]](#cite_note-231)

For instance, as Portuguese merchants were presumably the first to introduce the [sweet orange](/wiki/Sweet_orange "Sweet orange") in Europe, in several modern [Indo-European languages](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages") the fruit has been named after them. Some examples are Albanian [portokall](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/portokall#Albanian "wikt:portokall"), Bulgarian [портокал](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%D0%BF%D0%BE%D1%80%D1%82%D0%BE%D0%BA%D0%B0%D0%BB#Bulgarian "wikt:портокал") (*portokal*), Greek [πορτοκάλι](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CF%80%CE%BF%CF%81%CF%84%CE%BF%CE%BA%CE%AC%CE%BB%CE%B9#Greek "wikt:πορτοκάλι") (*portokali*), Macedonian [портокал](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%D0%BF%D0%BE%D1%80%D1%82%D0%BE%D0%BA%D0%B0%D0%BB#Macedonian "wikt:портокал") (*portokal*), Persian [پرتقال](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%D9%BE%D8%B1%D8%AA%D9%82%D8%A7%D9%84#Persian "wikt:پرتقال") (*porteghal*), and Romanian [portocală](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/portocal%C4%83#Romanian "wikt:portocală").[[231]](#cite_note-plantname-232)[[232]](#cite_note-OstergrenBosse2011-233) Related names can be found in other languages, such as Arabic [البرتقال](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%A8%D8%B1%D8%AA%D9%82%D8%A7%D9%84#Arabic "wikt:البرتقال") (*bourtouqal*), [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_language "Georgian language") [ფორთოხალი](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E1%83%A4%E1%83%9D%E1%83%A0%E1%83%97%E1%83%9D%E1%83%AE%E1%83%90%E1%83%9A%E1%83%98#Georgian "wikt:ფორთოხალი") (*p'ort'oxali*), Turkish [portakal](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/portakal#Turkish "wikt:portakal") and [Amharic](/wiki/Amharic "Amharic") *birtukan*.[[231]](#cite_note-plantname-232) Also, in [southern Italian dialects](/wiki/Regional_Italian "Regional Italian") (e.g., [Neapolitan](/wiki/Neapolitan_language "Neapolitan language")), an orange is *[portogallo](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/portogallo "wikt:portogallo")* or *[purtuallo](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/it:purtuallo "wikt:it:purtuallo")*, literally "(the) Portuguese (one)", in contrast to [standard Italian](/wiki/Standard_Italian "Standard Italian") *arancia*.

In light of its international importance, Portugal and Brazil are leading a movement to include Portuguese as one of the [official languages of the United Nations](/wiki/Official_languages_of_the_United_Nations "Official languages of the United Nations").[[233]](#cite_note-234)
